VETERAN FOCUSED INTEGRATION PROCESS (VIP) AND PRODUCT LINE MANAGEMENT (PLM)

The Contractor shall support VA efforts IAW the updated Veteran-Focused Integration Process (VIP) and Product Line Management (PLM) Playbook. The major focus of VIP is on Enterprise Oversight, Governance, reporting, and establishing practices that focus on outcomes and continuously delivering sustainable IT capabilities to benefit the Veteran. PLM is a framework that focuses on delivering functional products that provide the highest priority work to customers while delivering simplified, reliable, and practical solutions to the business, medical staff, and our Veterans. The VIP Guide is a companion guide to the PLM Transformation Playbook and can be found at: https://www.voa.va.gov/DocumentView.aspx?DocumentID=4371 and the PLM Transformation Playbook can be found at https://www.voa.va.gov/DocumentView.aspx?DocumentID=4946. VIP and PLM are the authoritative processes that IT projects must follow to ensure development and delivery of IT products.

The PLM Transformation Playbook details how OIT leadership expects Product Lines (PLs) to implement PLM, DevOps, and Lean-Agile principles, methods, practices, and techniques through levels of maturity. The PLM Transformation Playbook applies to all Information Technology (IT) products aligned to PLs in the Software Product Management, Infrastructure Operations and Product Engineering organizations within the OIT organization.

The primary objective of this award is to continue to provide support, maintenance, and enhancements to the TruData Modernization Data Platform. TruData Modernization will become the sole data platform that will form the backbone of FMCSA's IT modernization. FMCSA CTO, in collaboration with OCIO and other FMCSA leaders has set a strategy to build a Data Platform that will include a Data Lake, Data Warehouses and Data Marts that will enable efficient data storage, exchange and analysis for improving the registration, inspection, compliance and enforcement mission functions for FMCSA. The TruData Modernization effort will also streamline data exchanges between mission systems and eliminate redundant capabilities delivered by SAFER, SAFETYNET and other data structures implemented solely for the purpose of information sharing between systems. A continued innovative technical approach is needed to fully implement the data platform for FMCSA and realize the objectives outlined in TruData Modernization Bridge SOO. Specifically: The Contractor shall provide non-personal, IT systems support, staffing controls, program management, and shall deliver services both on and off-site as necessary to execute against all requirements established in the SOO and as set forth by the Government. All development and testing will occur within DOT/FMCSA environments, or instances of environments licensed to DOT/FMCSA, unless explicitly permitted by the COR in writing. Continue the development process with the entire contractor and government team that: develops end-to-end capability in an agile manner; facilitates open communication through regular check-ins and collaborative sessions; and provides clear actors/activities/documents/outcomes.

C.3.8.4 Software Development and Technical Labor Support

The contractor shall provide Software Development and Technical Labor Support for the NextGen Passport Personalization Printers within the Non-Production Environment (NPE), passport operations, and support for CA service desk tickets. 

The contractor shall: 

Provide support for the Print Controller software to successfully communicate with the TDIS. 

Develop and support the application to enable Passport staff to perform Government Acceptance Testing (GAT) for personalization printers in a standalone environment independent of the Print Controller and TDIS. This application will have an easy-to-use graphical user interface (GUI) and can run on a separate PC or laptop that can be connected directly to the personalization printers. The application will be able to accept the passport print jobs in XML format, perform chip and coding, engrave on the personalization page, and print the endorsement page.

Work with the Government to establish virtual development and testing environments on the NPE.

Fix performance, security, and code issues identified by the Government in NPE. The Government plans to use static code analysis to identify issues and will work with the Contractor to determine required fixes before moving to IV&V.

Design and develop new functions based on new user stories, e.g., encryption, page refresh, and labeling.

Develop comprehensive test cases and test scenarios, and perform end to end integration, usability and performance testing working with the support site location team to ensure robustness of software, and highest quality and accurate production of passport books.

Assist in supporting GitLab updates, logging defects in ELM, and work with CA Security in scanning and remediation, and enhancing any user level documentation.

C.3.4 Development Methodologies
The Contractor shall support a Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA), a flexible set of design principles applied during system development and integration phases, as specified at the TOs level.

Any SOA-based architecture shall be deployed on a secure, scalable, interoperable, and dynamic platform that provides end-to-end visibility and manageability – from application services through networking components – and supports use across multiple domains.

For HUD-specific TOs, the Contractor shall support HUD efforts in accordance with the following framework and processes:

Visual Innovation Platform (VIP) and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM):
 VIP enhances collaboration and decision-making through visual tools (e.g. digital concept boards, etc.).
 PLM serves as a central hub for data, connecting engineering, design, manufacturing, and supply chain teams.
 VIP and PLM work together to integrate workflows and drive digital transformation:
o VIP connects to PLM, pulling real-time product data to create interactive visual workspaces.
o Teams use VIP's visual boards to ideate, review, and make decisions, which then updates the central PLM system.
 This integration bridges strategic vision (VIP) with operational execution (PLM), accelerating innovation and information technology (IT) maturity.

The Contractor shall perform duties consistent with processes defined in the HUD Process Asset Library (PAL), a centralized digital repository that stores and shares process-related resources (templates, guidelines, policies, checklists, lessons learned, and metrics).

For HUD-specific TOs, the Contractor shall follow the HUD PAL, PLM, and VIP processes to ensure compliance with HUD policies, regulations, and quality standards. The Contractor shall use an incremental development methodology such as Agile (e.g. Scrum, Kanban, etc.), unless otherwise specified at the TOs level.

C.3.1.5 Project Management 

The Contractor shall provide project schedules to be integrated with CA/CST's Integrated Master Schedule (IMS). IMS aggregates individual schedule inputs from multiple projects to aid sequencing project tasks in an optimal order to meet CA/CST's overall priorities. The IMS is a vehicle for analysis and management of dependencies between projects, resource utilization, identification of resource constraints, and prioritization of activities across the enterprise.

Note: Management of the CST IMS is not within the scope of this contract. However, submission of project status into the IMS is a requirement and within scope. The Contractor shall maintain and update each project schedule to reflect progress after each COR/GTM status update, review, or milestone change. 

CST's SDLC process and approach for IT project management and execution incorporates SAFe Agile practices. Industry experience has shown these practices, when implemented correctly, produce higher quality products. CA has adopted a SAFe Agile approach for operations, maintenance, and engineering projects, and expects operations of CA/CST services to flow from this approach. The Contractor shall adhere to the CA/CST practices and guidelines accordingly.

THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY ELEMENTS

To consistently track these metrics and realize the above intent, the rest of this document provides a set of elements NGA and contractor teams must meet while delivering software products. These metrics and elements, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," ensure each software product has the people, processes, and tools in place to effectively operate at NGA. They apply to any team building a new product or modifying one already in service, whether making small changes, adding large new features, or creating entirely new products. The NGA Software Way applies to software products of all phases, including existing software products across the agency (not simply new products). Except where noted below, these elements must also be used when evaluating and implementing commercial software products for use at NGA. We understand that for large, existing software products, implementing The NGA Software Way won't happen all at once; it should start with tracking the metrics above and iteratively meeting each of the elements below.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users

Once user needs are deeply understood, the team must decide which of those needs to prioritize. To do this, the team must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. The product manager must break down the MVP into actionable work items that are easily understood by the entire team. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product.

Each day a product isn't actively solving user needs in production increases the risk the team is building the wrong thing. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years.

SPECIAL NOTICE FOR SB SET-ASIDE:
This RFP is a total small business set-aside. Offerors are strongly encouraged to ensure that the offeror is a small business based on the size standard for the NAICS Code assigned to this RFP. Reference 13 CFR Part 121, and the clauses and relevant FAR references contained within this solicitation. Ensure that your firm fits the definition including but not limited to any affiliations (domestic or foreign). The Small Business Administration determines whether an entity qualifies as a small business concern by counting its receipts, employees, or other measures including those of all its domestic and foreign affiliates, regardless of whether the affiliates are organized for profit. 13 C.F.R. 121.103(a)(6). SBA has a specific set of rules that explain when another person, business or entity is considered an affiliate for size standard purposes. SBA's rules on affiliation for its programs (except SBIR and STTR) are found at 13 C.F.R. 121.103.

M.1.1 The Government intends to award 100% Small Business Set-aside, Firm Fixed Price, Level of Effort (FFP/LOE) contract. An award will be made to the offeror whose proposal represents the best value to the Government in accordance with the criteria set forth below, and is eligible for award.

M.1.1.1 There are two (2) evaluation factors:
Volume 1 Staffing Plan Factor
Volume 2 Price Factor

The relative order of importance of these Factors are described in Section M.4 below.

M.1.1.2 Proposal selection will be made utilizing the source selection trade-off process, considering the evaluation Factors and their relative order of importance (see Section M.4), to identify the proposal which represents the best value and is most advantageous to the Government. The Government will weigh the evaluation factors (other than the Cost/Price Factor) against the final evaluated price to the Government. As part of the best value determination, the relative strengths, weaknesses, and risks of each proposal shall be considered in selecting the offer that is most advantageous and represents the best overall value to the Government. The Government reserves the right to choose not to award a contract if such action is in the Government's best interest.

The intended procurement is classified under NAICS code 541715 – Research and Development in Physical, Engineering, and Life Sciences with a Size Standard of 1000 employees.
This intended procurement will be a small business set-aside requirement.
REGULATORY AUTHORITY
This acquisition is conducted under the authority of the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Part 13—Simplified Acquisition Procedures. Contracts awarded using FAR Part 13—Simplified Acquisition Procedures are exempt from the requirements of FAR Part 6—Competition Requirements. FAR Subpart 13.106-1 (b) Soliciting from a single source provides that: For purchases not exceeding the simplified acquisition threshold, Contracting Officers may solicit from one source IF the contracting officer determines that the circumstances of the contract action deem only one source reasonably available (e.g., urgency, exclusive licensing agreements, brand-name OR industrial mobilization).

In accordance with FAR 15.503(a)(2), a SPECIAL NOTICE providing preaward notice to small business offerors will be posted to the GPE (SAM.gov) today, 5 March 2026, prior to the award of contracts pursuant to the CASS acquisition, which is anticipated for Wednesday, 11 March 2026. When using a small business set-aside, as here (see FAR Subpart 19.5), "the contracting officer shall notify each offeror in writing prior to award . . . ." FAR 15.503(a)(2). This preaward notification for small businesses allows small business offerors an opportunity to protest an apparent successful offeror's small-business size representation prior to award of a contract or immediately thereafter. See FAR 19.302(d). A size protest filed prior to notice being made of the selection of the apparent successful offeror(s) would be dismissed as premature by the Small Business Administration (SBA). FAR 19.302(d)(4). Upon receipt of the SPECIAL NOTICE identifying the apparent successful offeror(s), any concern or other interested party may file a protest with the contracting officer; however, in order to be considered "timely" it must be received by the contracting officer by the close of business of the fifth business day after the SPECIAL NOTICE is received. See FAR 19.302(d)(1). A protest must be timely in order to affect a specific solicitation. FAR 19.302(d).

L.2 Proposal Submission Tracks
Offerors must clearly identify which of the following two tracks they are competing under. An offeror may only submit a proposal for one track.
• Track 1: Unrestricted Competition (Full PWS Scope)
o This track is open to all businesses, regardless of size.
o Offerors under this track must submit a proposal that addresses all requirements across the entire Performance Work Statement (PWS).
o The Government intends to award a maximum of three (3) IDIQ contracts from this track.
• Track 2: Small Business Set-Aside (Partial PWS Scope)
o This track is a total small business set-aside. Only offerors certified as a small business under the applicable NAICS code for this acquisition may propose under this track.
o Offerors under this track may propose a minimum of one (1) and a maximum of four (4) major sections of the PWS. The major PWS sections are defined as: [List the major PWS sections here, e.g., 1. Program Management, 2. Engineering Support, 3. Cybersecurity, 4. Logistics, 5. Training].
o Offerors must explicitly state in their proposal which PWS sections they are proposing to perform. Their technical proposal, past performance, and price proposal must correspond only to the PWS sections selected.
o The Government intends to award a maximum of four (4) IDIQ contracts from this track.

Statement of Requirement
The horticulture specialist will assist RMA personnel and other interested parties to provide advice on nursery issues related to subtropical and temperate plants in areas covered by the 10 RMA Regional Offices. The Contractor shall perform the following tasks as detailed below:
(i) Advise, assist, and coordinate with RMA personnel and other interested parties on questions and issues related to nursery plant production practices, plant classification and taxonomy, hardiness zone limitations, storage and plant protection procedures and requirements, risk assessment, wholesale marketing, and nursery crop insurance procedures.
(ii) Attend RMA and nursery industry meetings, as necessary.
(iii) Aid and participate with RMA personnel in large claims, as necessary.
(iv) Possess knowledge of the NVS pilot crop insurance program to advise and assist RMA in modifications to the nursery program.

Oral Proposals: Directions for oral proposal elements are set forth below:

(a) See Table 1 in this RFP - Proposal Organization for Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach is to be proposed orally. Contractors shall submit slides of the planned oral proposal in accordance with the proposal due dates contained within this RFP. Slides shall be prepared using Microsoft PowerPoint, in any style the contractor deems reasonable; however, font size for slide text must be 14 points or larger. Font used for graphics shall be no smaller than ten points. 6. Term of Contract: The term of any resultant contract shall commence on the effective date indicated on page 1 and shall continue for a period of seven (7) years thereafter, unless terminated, cancelled or extended as otherwise provided herein.

8. Proposal Opening: Proposals shall be submitted no later than the due date and time indicated in the request for proposals. 9. Discussions: In accordance with the City of Peoria Procurement Code, after the initial receipt of proposals, discussions may be conducted with Offerors who submit proposals determined to be reasonably susceptible of being selected for award.

Instructions for Phase II Oral Presentation:
The Government's notification to offerors after evaluation of Phase I quote submissions will include the due date for Phase II written submissions, as well as the scheduled time for offerors oral presentations. The due date for Phase II written submissions will be prior to the scheduled oral presentations.

2. Oral Proposal. Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 90 minutes per offeror. At the conclusion of the oral proposal, the Government will convene for up to a 30-minute internal caucus to determine if there are any questions for the offeror. After the 30-minute caucus, there will be a 30-minute session for the Government to ask the offeror questions. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal, and offerors will not be given the opportunity to revise any part of their previously submitted written proposal, presentation slides, or submit any subsequent proposal revisions. The question-and-answer segment will be of clarifying nature only per FAR 15.306(a), which will not constitute discussions under FAR 15.306(b) and (d). Offerors may not exceed the 90-minute time limit for oral proposals.

3. Presentation Slides. Offerors must submit their presentation slides with their proposal submission for Subfactors 2.1 – 2.5 and must cover all presentation material. The slides must be received by the Contracting Officer via email no later than 48 hours prior to the scheduled presentation time. These slides will form part of the official record for evaluation. If the offeror does not include their slides, the Government will consider the offeror as non-responsive and exclude the offeror from further consideration.

4. Oral Presentation Participants. Offerors shall include a point of contact in their proposal for oral presentations, providing the point of contact's name, phone number, and email address. Each offeror is allowed four representatives at the oral presentation: three speakers (three key personnel or two key personnel and one company executive) and one silent participant.

Q2:  Will the Government accept a migration plan submitted as an appendix to the slide presentation and that the migration services strategy plan be evaluated as part of the proposal?

A2:  No.  The offeror must present its approach to integrate with existing DISA solutions and provide the migration services (outlined in PWS section 6.1.8) as part of their oral presentation.  

Q3: Can offerors submit external handouts for network diagrams outside of their slide presentation and not count against the maximum slide count? 

A3: No. The offeror must present the network design as part of their oral proposal.  The offeror may include network diagrams of the proposed design within the presentation slides as visual aids.  These designs will count toward the maximum slide count. 

Q4: Would DISA consider Oral and Technical Proposal submitted separately? Meaning, can Orals be assigned once down selected? 

A4: No. Also, there is no down selection process outlined in the RFP.

Q5: Will the slides for the Technical and Management Approach (Volume II) be due at the same time as Price volume and other elements of the full proposal?

A5: Yes; see the addendum to FAR 52.212-1(a)(7)(vi), Electronic Offers.  The offeror must submit all volumes with their proposal. 

Q6: How will offerors schedule / be notified of their Oral Presentation date and time? 

A6: See addendum to 52.212-1(a)(7)(vii)(B)(7). The Government intends to schedule oral proposals with responsive offerors approximately 10 business days after the solicitation closes. Oral proposals will take place approximately within 30 days after they are scheduled; dates and times will be assigned at random for all responsive offerors.

Q7: Are all offerors given an opportunity to present their technical approach orally via MS Teams following timely and accurate submission of the proposal?

A7: If an offeror's proposal is timely and complete (adheres to the RFP), the Government will invite the offeror to present their technical proposal orally.

Instructions for Phase II Oral Presentation:
The Government's notification to offerors after evaluation of Phase I quote submissions will include the due date for Phase II written submissions, as well as the scheduled time for offerors oral presentations. The due date for Phase II written submissions will be prior to the scheduled oral presentations.

The technical and management volume should be specific, complete and consist solely of the oral proposal and the offeror's slides used during the presentation. If there is a discrepancy between the offeror's slides and the oral proposal, the information in the slides shall take precedence. However, the Government will not evaluate or consider information referenced in the offeror's slides that was not presented orally as part of the offeror's proposal.

2. Oral Proposal. Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 90 minutes per offeror. At the conclusion of the oral proposal, the Government will convene for up to a 30-minute internal caucus to determine if there are any questions for the offeror. After the 30-minute caucus, there will be a 30-minute session for the Government to ask the offeror questions. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal, and offerors will not be given the opportunity to revise any part of their previously submitted written proposal, presentation slides, or submit any subsequent proposal revisions. The question-and-answer segment will be of clarifying nature only per FAR 15.306(a), which will not constitute discussions under FAR 15.306(b) and (d). Offerors may not exceed the 90-minute time limit for oral proposals.

These presentations will be scheduled for approximately seven business days after the submission of the Phase 2 written proposal and slides and will be assigned randomly.

All costs related to the oral presentations will be the responsibility of the offeror; no costs will be reimbursed by the Government.

5.1.2 Evaluation Criteria
Technical Proposals will be initially evaluated and scored by the College Evaluation Committee based on the following criteria:

1) Platform Functionality: Maximum Point Value=30
0-30 based on Offeror's platform functionality. Overall system capabilities and features, alignment with required technical specifications, user interface design and ease of navigation, customization and configuration options, Integration capabilities with existing systems, accessibility and compliance standards.

2) Qualifications: Maximum Point Value=20
0-20 points based on professional qualifications and technical competence of the firm, subcontractors, and staff proposed for the performance of the required services. Offeror's organization size, length of time the organization has been providing the required services.

3) Past Performance and Experience: Maximum Point Value=20
0-20 points based on Offeror having successfully engaged in professional services of this type for a minimum of five (5) years. Demonstrated success in projects similar to the one described in this RFP. Experience providing services to institutions or public-sector entities similar in size and mission to Montgomery College.

4) Product Demonstration: Maximum Point Value=10

5) Pricing: Maximum Point Value=20
Highest possible evaluation score=100 points

5.2.1.5 Finalist Presentation Evaluation

During the Technical Proposal evaluation process, the RFP Coordinator will use the RFP Attachment 6.4, Section C.5, to manage the Finalist Presentation Evaluations and maintain evaluation records.

The highest evaluated Proposers, based on the preliminary review of the Technical proposals, will be invited to participate in the Finalist Presentations phase.

Each Proposal Evaluation Team member will independently evaluate each Presentation against the Presentation criteria in this RFP, rather than against other presentations, and will score each in accordance with the defined topics in Section C.5. After completion of the Finalist Presentations, the evaluators will each finalize scoring of the Technical Proposals.

5.2.3 Cost Proposal Evaluation

After the Technical Proposal evaluation and Finalist Presentation evaluation has/have been completed, the Solicitation Coordinator will open the Cost Proposals and use the RFP Attachment 6.6, Cost Proposal and Scoring Guide to calculate and document the Cost Proposal scores.

5.2.4 Total Proposal Score

The Solicitation Coordinator will calculate the sum of the Technical Proposal scores, the Presentation scores, and the Cost Proposal scores and record the resulting number as the total score for the subject Proposal.

Pitch evaluation will consider the full content of the actual Oral Presentation (including answers or inability to answer any questions posed) and the content of the slides presented. No. Evaluation Criteria Scoring Method Weight (Points)
1. Quality of Engagement 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated based on their ability to engage and respond effectively to the committee's questions and comments.

2. Understanding of City Needs and System Integration 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on their demonstrated understanding of the City's operational needs and objectives, as well as their ability to integrate with existing utility systems. This includes compatibility with GIS platforms, SCADA/ADMS data ingestion, CIS, AMI, and IVR interfaces, and the ability to maintain data accuracy, synchronization, and minimal latency across systems.

3. Implementation Methodology and Project Management 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on the clarity and effectiveness of their implementation approach, including defined project phases and timeline, required utility resources, data migration and validation processes, and support for testing, cutover, and go-live activities to ensure a successful deployment.

4. Training, Support, and Lifecycle Services 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on their ability to provide comprehensive initial and ongoing training, a clearly defined support structure with appropriate coverage, escalation procedures, and response times, and a proactive approach to software updates, enhancements, and cybersecurity.

5. Presentation, Demonstration, Interview (10 Points)
Bidders that are susceptible to contract award based on their combined technical and cost score will be invited to provide a Presentation/Demonstration/Interview. UPSTATE will notify the Bidders and schedule the date and time. The demonstration will provide an opportunity for these bidders to clarify or elaborate on their proposals but shall in no way change their original proposals.

All demonstrations will be performed virtually utilizing Webex; UPSTATE will provide the Bidder with the connection information. Traveling to UPSTATE is neither required nor requested.

The demonstration will be evaluated and scored by the technical review committee. All costs associated with the Bidder's demonstration will be borne by the Bidder. For the agenda, see Attachment 12: Demonstration Agenda. At the demonstration there will be no discussion of costs or services that are not included in the Bidder's pricing or proposal. The demonstration shall not exceed ninety (90) minutes including questions.

The demonstration is worth a total of ten (10) points. Points from the demonstration will be added to Bidder's Technical and Cost scores to arrive at a Final Composite Score.

DEMONSTRATION REQUIREMENTS (MODIFIED)
Demonstrations given by an Offeror under this section are permitted and communication by the Offeror with the Agency or its employees during a demonstration will not violate the restrictions applicable to Offerors. The presentation will be held virtually, and any expenses incurred by the Offeror are the Offeror's sole responsibility. The time allotted per individual Offeror to present and demonstrate facts shall not exceed one hour, to include a question-and-answer session.

The Procurement Officer will notify the responsive Offerors as quickly as possible to schedule a specific date and time.

THIS IS NOT A MARKETING OPPORTUNITY!

(a) The activities of the Offeror are limited to a demonstration of the system proposed and described in the Offeror's written proposal.
(b) Evaluators may ask questions pertaining to the Offeror's demonstration at the conclusion. The Offeror's answers are restricted to statements of fact. The Offeror may be required to document an answer if such a written clarification is determined to be in the best interest of the State.
(c) Offeror will not be allowed or permitted to introduce new information or show products/features not included in their proposal.
(d) Negotiation is not permitted at this stage in the procurement process.
(e) The demonstration should be conducted in a straightforward manner in order to secure a clear and meaningful understanding of the Offeror's proposal.

STEP 2 Evaluation

Offerors invited to Step 2 will be evaluated through technical evaluation and price. No other criteria will be used. Technical evaluation is more important than price.

Technical
The Government will evaluate the offeror's solution proposed during its demonstration, including presentation material, against the evaluation criteria described below. The Government will use a Pass/Fail system for the first two evaluation criteria. If the Government renders the first two factors for the proposed solution as "Pass," the proposal will be evaluated against criteria three through five below, which are in descending order of importance. Each demonstration and supporting material shall be evaluated on its individual merits rather than on a comparative basis with other offerors.

Technical Factors:
• The proposed solution is relevant and directly addresses the problem statement, objectives, and requirements outlined in the selected Focus Area. (Pass/Fail)
• The offeror's proposed data rights assertions adequately meet the Government's stated Focus Area data-rights intent. (Pass/Fail)
• The extent to which the proposed solution possesses strong technical merit, is feasible, and is supported by a clear and credible plan for identifying, managing, and mitigating technical and programmatic risks.
• The extent to which the offeror demonstrates company viability, possesses a qualified management team, and has provided a sound and acceptable notional schedule for project execution.
• The extent to which the solution presents a unique, underutilized, or innovative application of commercial technology, products, or practices that provides a compelling advantage over traditional approaches.

SECTION 4 – PITCHES (PHASE 2)

4.1 General Guidelines
In Phase 2, companies shall provide an in-person pitch at their proposed location or other location agreed upon by the company and the Government to demonstrate and provide further details on their proposed concept / technology / solution and the technical and business viability of the proposed solution. The Government may also leverage external market research during Phase 2 to evaluate a company's viability.

In addition to the Pitch, the Government, at its discretion, may request for consideration an additional written submission to further supplement or clarify the information provided in the Phase 1 Solution Briefs. The Government, at its discretion, may elect to release additional information to companies that have been selected for the pitch.

The pitch format will be as follows:
(1) Up to a 90-minute presentation provided by the company, the Government may request an audio recording of this presentation
(2) Up to a 90-minute question and answer between the Government and company
(3) Up to a 120-minute site visit / tour of facilities by company, if the company location is selected

Individual pitches will be evaluated based on the merit of the proposed concept in addressing the SOW.

4.2 Pitch Content
During the Phase 2 Pitch, the company must detail / address at a minimum:
• Organizational breakdown
   o Explain the team and major stakeholder structure and subject matter expertise within the team.
• Technology Solution
   o Overview of the attributes which will be met by the solution.
   o Design maturity and past operational history (if applicable). If not applicable, detail the approach to overcome this potential barrier.

4.3 Pitch Basis of Evaluation
Individual pitches will be evaluated against the following factors:
(1) Relevance of the solution in addressing the proposed portions of the SOW and the requirements in this document;
(2) The solution has technical merit, adequately addresses Government need and is feasible;
(3) Evaluation of company viability;
(4) Company's approach is a compelling solution to the Government problem;
(5) The cost estimate and details behind this estimate;
(6) A notional production schedule is acceptable; and
(7) The potential impact of data rights assertions.

Upon completion of the Pitch phase evaluations, the Government will notify the Company that:
(1) the proposed concept/technology/solution has been selected for award of an OTA; or
(2) the proposed concept/technology/solution is not of interest to the Government.

SECTION 5 – PROTOTYPE PROJECT AWARDS (PHASE 3)

5.1 General Guidelines
After award of the OTA, the Government may issue the Company a Request for Project Proposal (RPP) for a project-specific proposal in accordance with a Collaborative Task Instruction (CTI). Receipt of an RPP does not guarantee that a Company will receive a prototype project award. The Government reserves the right, at any point prior to award, to cancel the RPP.

If an RPP is provided, the Company will be invited to develop and submit its proposal. Companies may discuss ideas and details of the proposal during the proposal writing process with the Government. Specifics on a CTI will be provided at the time of RPP.

Upon favorable review and available funds, the Government may choose to make a prototype project award to the Agreement Holder.

I.1. Summary. HQ SACT is issuing this Request for Innovative Participation (RFIP) announcement in order to facilitate collaboration between the HQ SACT, SHAPE (Supreme Headquarters Allied Powers Europe) and participants regarding solutions. The purpose of this RFIP is to request participants to submit solutions (either existing and/or under development) to the scenario presented within Annex I and so participate in the NATO Innovation Challenge 2026-1 (iChall26-1). All submissions must conform to this RFIP.

A Selection Committee composed of representatives from HQ SACT and other relevant experts invited by HQ SACT (the "reviewers") will select up to 10 solutions (hereinafter as "the Finalists") amongst the responses submitted. The Finalists will participate in the iChall26-1 Pitch Day projected to be May 28, 2026, in Rennes, FRANCE. Participation in the Pitch Day is only open to in-person. During the Pitch Day, a Jury composed of representatives from HQ SACT and other relevant experts invited by HQ SACT (the "Jury Members") will assess which of the 10 solutions best respond to the scenario presented in Annex I and select the iChall26-1 winners.

I.2. Dates. The event and timelines of iChall26-1 are described in Section III. It will take place between 10 March and 28 May 2026, with the iChall26-1 Pitch Day taking place on-site on 28 May 2026.

Criteria for Selection
Responsive proposals submitted in response to this RFP will be evaluated by a committee comprised of regional tourism partners and the Corporation's staff. Each committee member will independently review and score proposals based on the Evaluation Criteria set forth below. Following individual scoring, the committee will convene to discuss the proposals and determine the highest-scoring proposals.

The proposals receiving the highest total scores may be invited, at the Corporation's discretion, to participate in an oral presentation. The Corporation anticipates that up to three top-ranked Proposers may be asked to provide an oral presentation to the committee, at their own expense, at the Corporation's office in Providence, Rhode Island. Presentations may alternatively be conducted virtually at the sole discretion of the Corporation. In-person presentations shall include no more than three representatives from the Proposer's team, including management and key personnel responsible for day-to-day implementation.

Proposers will be asked to demonstrate the dashboard product and a representative dataset that supports the Corporation's tourism strategic planning efforts. A question-and-answer period will follow each presentation. Oral presentations are intended to clarify and respond to questions regarding the written proposals but will not affect the overall final score of the written proposals. Final scores and rankings will be based on the Committee's evaluation of the written proposals alone.

Oral Presentations shall not exceed 40 minutes.

A 25-minute interactive Question and Answer (Q&A) period between the Government evaluation team and the Proposer to address questions from the Government evaluation team.

Technology Challenges: Identify specific approaches to solving the technology challenges identified in Section 2.3.6. in order to achieve the stated program metrics. Technical Ability: Detail the specific qualifications and experience that position the Proposer's organization and proposed team to successfully achieve the goals of the CyPhER Forge program. M.3 Award Decision
The Government will make awards to the offerors representing the best value within each track.
• Track 1 (Unrestricted): The Government will rank the proposals based on the evaluation factors. The Source Selection Authority (SSA) will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.
• Track 2 (Small Business Set-Aside): The Government will separately rank the proposals within this track. The SSA will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.

M.1 Basis for Award
This is a competitive best value tradeoff source selection. The evaluation process will be conducted in two phases. Phase I will consist of the evaluation of the written proposals. Phase II will consist of Oral Presentations for those offerors determined to be in the competitive range. The final evaluation will incorporate the results of both phases.
Factor 1 (Technical) is significantly more important than Factor 2 (Past Performance). Factors 1 and 2, when combined, are significantly more important than Factor 3 (Cost/Price). As the non-cost/price factors become more equal among offerors, the importance of Cost/Price will increase.

Award shall be made to the responsible proposer whose overall technical and financial proposal was evaluated as best meeting the needs of the University as set forth in the RFP.
Proposals received in response to this solicitation will be evaluated by MSU using source selection procedures. The award will be based upon an assessment of each offeror's ability to satisfy the solicitation requirements. Relative Importance:

Factor 1: (Technical Approach) is more important than Factor 2: (Management Approach), Factor 2: (Management Approach) is more important than Factor 3: (Oral Presentation), Factor 3: (Oral Presentation) is more important than Factor 4: (Past Performance). 3.1 Guardian Automation Layer (GAL): The GAL will serve as the Single User Interface for Guardians and HR Practitioners to interact with military HR Data. Historically, Guardian data has been stored within disparate systems, making process improvements and automation cumbersome. With no single source of truth for Guardian records or port-of-call for completing military HR actions, practitioners and end users rely on manual workarounds to reach data needed for decision making.

The primary purpose of the Space Force GAL is to track and manage Guardians in a service without component structure throughout the military HR lifecycle. The desired solution will allow the Guardian a continuous experience within the GAL throughout the entirety of the HR lifecycle, from onboarding and career development to retirement and beyond. The GAL will be flexible, with a positive user experience based in human-centered design practices that are reflective of modern commercial capabilities.

The USSF is pursuing a solution that leverages role-based authorities within a unified interface to analyze data and create interactive dashboards. Low code/no code configurability will allow practitioners to adapt to the evolving policy and process requirements, such as PMA.

Based on current market research, interoperability and system constraints, it has been determined that only brand-name managed services can fully meet and satisfy the requirement. Only the specific OEM proprietary, brand-name specific MSCB, which is available through authorized resellers, will satisfy the needs and can support the infrastructures currently in place. This requirement is for proprietary products and/or product support, and no competitor functional replacement products exist in the market at this time to meet the Government's needs.

This requirement cannot be set aside for a small business or any other socio-economic group because the ostensible subcontract rule, 13 C.F.R. 121.103(h)(3) would apply. With the required MSCB solution, Menlo Security, Inc., becomes a subcontractor. In addition, subcontracting IAW FAR 52.219-14, Limitations on Subcontracting, cannot be met since a small business would not be providing over 50% of the cost of the requirement.

The Government will continue to conduct market research to determine if there are changes in the marketplace that would enable future competitive actions; i.e., there are similar products and/or product support in the marketplace that provides the same functionality, the original equipment manufacturer releases ownership rights of the product, or it is economically feasible to procure a new system.

There will be an initial 2.5-year contract period with the option for two (2) additional 3-year extensions at the discretion of the Division. The Division is seeking to lease and receive support services for a BSTS and compatible AVS as defined in this Scope of Services. Two (2) high-speed tabulators are also required for the state's RCV central count and RLA processes. The successful Bidder will provide election management software to allow the Division to independently create ballots and program removable memory devices for use with the tabulators and AVS. The term of the lease will be for an initial 2.5-year period, with the option to renew for two 3-year extensions at the discretion of the Division.

General Dispatch Support Services includes access to on-line software, web-based tools/applications optimized for specific aircraft roles, iOS applications for use on approved tablet/Electronic Flight Bag (EFB), and the automation of the computations associated with aircraft specific flight/mission planning. Once the mission planning information has been generated, it will be made available in deliverable digital format (i.e. PDF) for display on EFBs and other mobile devices including iPads, as well as aircraft navigation systems. Some Dispatch Support Services include functions to transmit and receive flight/mission information via satellite datalink communications, either at the commencement of a flight/mission, or as real time updates throughout a flight/mission. Dispatch Support Services may also be used for post-flight/mission debriefing. These Dispatch Support Services are required for aircraft assigned to Commander, Naval Air Force Reserve (CNAFR) Types/Models/Series of the aircraft assigned include the K/C-130J (12ea), the KCC-130T (25ea); the C-40A (19ea); the UC-35C/D (3ea), and the UC-12F/W (4ea), for a total of sixty-three (63) aircraft.

Strategic Acquisition Center (SAC), on behalf of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), Veterans Health Administration (VHA), Office of Informatics (OHI), intends to award a non-competitive, firm fixed price, sole source contract to Oracle Health Government Services, Inc. This procurement is for a platform specific training to provide informatics support for both the VistA-based and Oracle Health-based Federal Electronic Health Record (EHR) and associated tools to monitor VistA and Federal EHR use, configuration, and quality. This training is a discreet need from the VA-specific Oracle Health training provided to VHA staff at go live sites by VA's Electronic Health Record Modernization – Integration Office (EHRM-IO). The estimated award date is April 15, 2026. The proposed procurement is for services for which the Government intends to utilize other than full and open competition for this procurement is 41 USC 3304(a)(1) as implemented by the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Subpart 6.103-1 entitled, 'Only on responsible source and no other supplies or services will satisfy agency requirements', 41 USC 3304(a)(1) permits agencies may contract without providing for full and open competition when the supplies or services required by the agency are available from only one responsible source and no other type of supplies or services will satisfy agency requirements.

1. INTRODUCTION. This document defines the materials, training, and support needed to provide advanced training on the Oracle Health Electronic Health Record (EHR) platform and associated tools to the Veterans Health Administration (VHA) for administration of Health Informatics roles and responsibilities.

1.1. Background. VHA provides informatics support for both the VistA-based and Oracle Health-based Federal EHR and associated tools to monitor VistA and Federal EHR use, configuration, and quality. VHA staff members who are or will be responsible for such support during and following the Federal EHR deployment require training on the Oracle Health platform and tooling architecture.

1.2. Oracle Health maintains a catalog of training opportunities available to VA's EHRM-IO and all Oracle Health clients. Training opportunities include instructor-led, virtual instructor-led, and self-paced online courses. Online courses range from 15 minutes to several hours. Instructor-led and virtual instructor-led courses range from 3 hours to 8 days. The training opportunities and catalog include per-course pricing ranging from approximately $200 to $4,850. Oracle Health also offers a 'Premier Pass' option, which allows a holder of the 'Premier Pass' to enroll in as many courses as they wish during the period of performance of the pass (typically, one fiscal year).

Cherokee County currently utilizes the CityView software platform to administer and manage permitting, licensing, inspections, plat review, and related regulatory functions across multiple departments. These functions include, but are not limited to, building permits, land development permits, subdivision plat applications, trade permits, business licensing, inspection scheduling, code enforcement case management, and associated regulatory reporting. CityView serves as the central system of record for regulatory workflows, subdivision plat routing and review processes, approval tracking, condition management, and reporting activities. The system supports both internal staff operations and public-facing services. Over time, Cherokee County has experienced continued residential and commercial growth, increased development activity, and rising service demands. As a result, application volumes, inspection workloads, and data management requirements have expanded significantly. While the current system has supported County operations, the County is evaluating its long-term sustainability and alignment with evolving operational needs. Areas under consideration include scalability, configurability, integration with enterprise systems (including financial and GIS platforms), user experience for both staff and the public, reporting and analytics capabilities, vendor support structure, and overall total cost of ownership.

B. Quality of Service
At a minimum, the following information must be provided in the proposal package:
1. An exact listing of new equipment to be provided, installed and maintained by the contractor and evidence to the fact that all equipment and software is operational, proven and in current use. It is important to detail the system capacity and software capabilities as well as alternative methods of purchase in lieu of traditional in-person sales.
2. Inventory current seating capacity and generate an electronic seating manifest to be kept current with any future renovations.
3. A full description of the software including back-up procedures and disaster recovery capabilities.
4. Direct related experience: provide a description of the company and its directly related experience including references.
5. Marketing programs: provide a detailed marketing and promotional plan aimed at increasing awareness and ticket sales at the Sports Complex through database marketing, email blasts, etc.
6. Alternative ticketing purchasing capabilities: proposal shall list alternative methods from traditional in-person sales for ticket purchasing including internet and smart phone applications. All alternative methods for purchase operations should be explained in detail including methods in development for future application as well as approach to adjust to market trends.
8. The contractor will be responsible for all activities related to installation of the system and the initial and ongoing training of all employees. Contractor must provide a timeline for future system and equipment upgrades and replacements.
9. An estimated timeline and implementation action plan from the time the order is placed to a fully functional system. Include set-up, installation, staff training, etc.

I. Equipment
1. Proposer shall provide equipment for twenty-two (22) point-of-sale workstations to include computer, monitor, keyboard, credit card swipes, and ticket printer at each workstation.
2. Proposer shall be able to provide a computer printing capability of printing at least thirty-five tickets per minute at twenty-two point of sale locations. Ticket printer must be quiet, thermal, and shall be able to have the print size, ticket format, ticket cutter control, and image darkness or lightness adjusted by the terminal operator through easy to use commands.
3. Ticket Scanning/Access Manager equipment must be provided and have the ability for instant accept or decline entry notification. See Exhibit A for Peoria Stadium gate configuration for access control communication points. Each gate listed on Exhibit A will have one or two access control antennaes based on technical capabilities of contractors equipment. The contractor will provide twenty (20) access control handheld scanners and appropriate charging stations to house all scanners.
4. The capacity of the system must be designed for growth and be fully upgradeable. The system must be able to accept new terminals simply and conveniently. With little or no change to the central processor configuration, additional peripheral devices should be able to be brought online.
5. Software applications should be Windows based or comparable system that is user friendly and should allow for timely application updates.
6. System shall be able to provide remote or VPN access to ticketing system and information for ticket office management.
7. Contractor must provide one (1) Automated Ticketing Kiosk either free-standing or window-inserted upon request of the City based on business needs.

The North Carolina Department of Labor (NCDOL) is seeking information from qualified vendors regarding solutions to modernize and replace its existing Elevator and Amusement Device inspection and permitting systems. The current systems have been in place for over 15 years and no longer adequately support modern business processes, data integration, or reporting needs. NCDOL is pursuing a comprehensive, scalable solution that supports end-to-end operations including: Device inspection request, Permit submission and approval, Inspection scheduling and execution, Reporting and compliance tracking, Invoicing and financial system integration. This RFI is issued for informational and planning purposes only. Responses will be used to inform a potential future Request for Proposals (RFP). NCDOL's existing systems present several operational and technical limitations, including: Reliance on legacy technologies with limited flexibility, Manual and fragmented workflows, Limited integration with financial and external systems, Insufficient reporting and analytics capabilities, Lack of mobile and offline functionality for inspectors. Vendors are encouraged to address how their proposed solutions resolve these challenges.

BACKGROUND: Central Arkansas Veterans Healthcare System (CAVHS) has a requirement to upgrade the SPECTRALIS Software (HEYEX2).

SCOPE: HEYEX 2 is equipment that captures high-resolution, cross-sectional images of the retina and optic nerve, which allows providers to diagnose and treat retina and glaucoma-related diseases and conditions.

SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS:

Scalability: HEYEX 2 can scale from a small practice with a single device to a large enterprise with hundreds of imaging devices across multiple sites.

Database: It supports an SAP® SQL database for stability and scalability, which is essential for managing large volumes of data.

User Interface: HEYEX 2 features a robust clinical tool set that allows efficient data pre-processing and simultaneous access to patient records by multiple users.

Integration: The platform interfaces with Electronic Health Records (EHR) systems, eliminating duplicate entries and streamlining workflow.

Security: HEYEX 2 is designed with cybersecurity features to ensure the security of patient data and images.

TRAINING: The eye clinic already has this system at the stations; they just need to update the software and firmware.

Vendor Certification
Integrated Web-based Electronic Health Record ("EHR") and Practice Management System
RFP# 260028

As of January 1, 2005, the Office of the State Comptroller is requiring that governmental agencies award contracts only to vendors that have been certified as "responsible." Vendor responsibility means that a vendor has the integrity to justify the award of public dollars and the capacity to fully perform the requirements of the contract. It is a contracting agency's responsibility, under Section 63 (9) of the State Finance Law (SFL), to evaluate and make a determination of the responsibility of a prospective contractor. A responsibility determination, wherein the contracting agency determines that it has reasonable assurances that a vendor is responsible, is an important part of the procurement process, promoting fairness in contracting and protecting a contracting agency and the State (as well as the County) against failed contracts.

The following factors are to be considered in making a responsibility determination:
1. Legal Authority to do business in New York State
2. Integrity
3. Capacity - both organizational and financial
4. Previous performance

Please complete the enclosed Vendor Responsibility Questionnaire. The completed Questionnaire shall be returned with your bid submission in order for your bid to be ruled responsive.

SOLE SOURCE JUSTIFICATION FOR THE PURCHASE OF CYBERFEDS
This letter is to certify that LRP Publications, Inc., is the sole source of the unique combination of case decisions, statutes, regulations, manuals and guides, pay tables, bulletins and newsgroups. These research and analytical tools comprise cyberFEDS® on the Web.

Below is a list of resources that are available exclusively on cyberFEDS® on the Web:
1. Quick Start Guides (QSGs) — provide overviews of more than 260 EEO, employee relations, labor relations, classification and staffing topics and links to noteworthy cases, statutes, regulations and additional resources.
2. Summaries of Case Decisions and Arbitration Awards — permits users to quickly scan search results and call up the full text if further detail is desired.
3. Case Index Numbering System — a copyrighted system that enables users to speed and focus their research.
4. Forms Library — contains standard forms, optional forms, OPM forms, FLRA & FMCS forms, OGE forms and more.
5. News and Legislation — read the latest articles posted daily broken out by topic or search the News Archive.

For these reasons, this acquisition is being pursued as a sole source.

SCOPE
A. The work of this section includes furnishing all water meters with MIUs and encoder register and a complete radio frequency based Automated Meter Reading (AMR) System or radio frequency based Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) System. The system shall include furnish of water meters and registers, radio frequency components, data collection equipment and data collection software and training. The proposed system shall be implemented on new and existing residential, commercial, industrial and governmental customers.

B. The meter reading system shall consist of the necessary hardware and software to allow automatic meter readings and other data to be sent from the meter to a computer, handheld unit or internet, capable of downloading into the Owner's billing system. This automatic meter reading system equipment shall be furnished under this Contract, and installed by others. All meters, encoder registers and communications equipment will be furnished as part of this contract.

C. Training for the water meters and meter reading system will not be conducted until all water meters and appurtenances are installed as part of a separate installation contract.

REQUIRED EXPERIENCE

B. Experience managing similar projects of this magnitude and provide a minimum of three references.

C. Approved system suppliers are Mueller, Badger, Neptune, Sensus, Zenner or equal. One manufacturer shall supply both metering equipment (water meters) and radios (encoder registers with MIUs).

As part of this effort, FEMA has selected Salesforce as its enterprise Customer Relationship Management (CRM) platform for Individual Assistance case management and survivor engagement. Future IA capabilities, including insurance verification and data-sharing, are expected to integrate with and leverage this Salesforce-based environment and FEMA's broader IA technical architecture.

5. Can the Government give examples of and more context or provide a clarifying definition on what may be deemed "significant development": Existing and adaptable commercial or other solution that meets or exceeds all objectives identified in Appendix 001 without what the Government interprets to be a significant development. For example, would integrating into a satellite and launching an existing (ie, "sitting on the shelf") instrument considered be "significant development"?
NOAA Response: In this context, significant development is intended to include technology maturation and operational readiness. For instance, an existing on-orbit capability may require less development than an assembled and tested instrument that has not been launched, which in turn requires less maturation than an instrument concept.

The City of Fort Lauderdale is requesting proposals from qualified vendors for the purchase of a Mobile Integrated Health Software. The City seeks a Mobile Integrated Health platform that will provide an efficient and streamlined process for capturing necessary data, supporting patient advocacy, incorporating new technology, and ensuring appropriate follow-up of client encounters. The City is looking for an all-in-one solution to provide the following minimum requirements. Software platform shall be NEMSIS (National Emergency Medical Services Information System) compliant. Software platform shall be EMSTARS (EMS Tacking and Reporting System), the Florida reporting system approved. Software platform should not be incident based, but rather have a true longitudinal patient record. Software platform shall have telehealth capabilities with secure, HIPAA-compliant access integrated into the patient's record for future billing and reimbursement. Software platform shall be able to assess and document MIH encounters, including but not limited to mental health, substance use disorder, chronic disease management, fall risk, depression, recovery, and other clinical domains. Software platform shall be able to securely share access and information for patient referrals and task collaboration with other first responders, physicians/providers, and community services. Software platform is compliant with HIPAA, HITECH, 42 CFR Part 2 within a single fully integrated system. Software platform utilizes AI tools to increase efficiency and assist in diagnosis of chronic health issues. Software platform shall be accessible across all devices and operating systems, including but not limited to iPads, iPhones, Android, MS Surface Tablets, and Laptops.

5. SECTION B - SCOPE OF SERVICES:
5.1. Background
The Office of the County Attorney (OCA) provides legal advice and services to County departments, agencies, boards and commissions, as well as the County Council. Currently there are about 55 staff attorneys and 25 legal support staff to handle legal issues, and provide opinions, prepare and present legislation, negotiate and prepare agreements and other documents on behalf of the County, draft resolutions and ordinances, acquire and convey title to real property, and represent the County in court and before various administrative bodies.
As counsel to one of the largest and most populous Counties in Maryland, the Office of the County Attorney collects and generates huge amounts of electronic data on a daily basis. This includes the emails, documents and many other forms of electronically stored information ("ESI") that are often used in the discovery phase during litigation and investigations as well as for responding to Maryland Public Information Act ("MPIA") requests. Annually, OCA handles an average of 230 lawsuits and 60 Maryland Public Information Act requests.
OCA requires the capability to effectively manage and review ESI during litigation and to efficiently respond to MPIA requests.
5.2. Intent
OCA is seeking proposals from qualified vendors for the provision of a cloud-based electronic discovery ("e-discovery") Software as a Service (SaaS) solution that will allow OCA to collect, store, process, analyze, review and produce ESI as part of discovery in litigation or in response to MPIA requests.

The selected vendor shall provide a "turn-key" solution including all necessary hardware, cloud-based software, implementation services, and ongoing support. The system must be accessible via district-issued student 1:1 devices (Chromebooks/iPads/MacBooks/Windows) and staff workstations.

A. Student & Teacher Workflow
● Request & Approval: Students must be able to request a pass from their device; teachers must have a "one-click" approval/denial interface.
● Auto-Expiration: Passes must feature a countdown timer. If a student exceeds the allotted time (e.g., 5 minutes for a restroom), an automated alert must be sent to designated staff.
● Kiosk Mode: Support for "Quick-Pass" stations (dedicated tablets) in classrooms or common areas for students without devices.

B. Safety & Conflict Mitigation
● Encounter Prevention: The ability for administrators to "lock" specific student pairs or groups so they cannot have active passes simultaneously.
● Room/Area Capacity: Real-time limits on how many students can be in a specific location (e.g., "Max 3 students in North Restroom").
● Restricted Zones: Ability to "close" certain hallways or restrooms during specific periods (e.g., lunch or passing periods).

C. Administrative Monitoring
● Live Hallway Dashboard: A real-time "active pass" list for security and administrators to see every student currently in the hallway, their destination, and time remaining.
● Emergency Mode: A "Global Override" to instantly cancel all active passes and notify students to return to their classrooms during a lockdown or drill.

1.1. General Requirements
The proposed Asset Management System (AMS) must be a stable, scalable, and single platform solution with the capacity to integrate with existing city applications. The proposer must demonstrate the ability to meet or exceed the following specifications:
• Cost Transparency: Provide detailed comments regarding costs, alternatives, and any other pertinent information.
• GIS-Centricity: Must utilize the existing authoritative Enterprise ArcGIS geodatabase for all asset data (eliminating data redundancy).
• Asset Lifecycle Management: Ability to track asset inventory, condition, performance, risk analysis, and remaining useful life for long-term planning and capital improvement.
• Work Order Management (CMMS): Full functionality for service requests, preventive maintenance, corrective maintenance, inspections, and ad-hoc work orders.
• Mobile Functionality: Highly functional, easy-to-use mobile application for field crews (online/offline capabilities, GPS/mapping features, data capture).
• Must be a hosted off-site and capable of consuming Realtime ArcGIS web services.
• Must integrate with the Civic Plus, See-click-Fix application.
• Must be a business partner with PipeLogix Inc. and able to integrate with PipeLogix Phoenix application.
• Must be able to integrate with hosted Open Gov Financials application.
• Must be able to integrate with the Corps of Engineers' MicroPaver 'Paver' application.
• Must be capable of integrating with the My Government Online (MGO) permitting, code enforcement and land management online platform.
• Active Directory Integration (SAML) for 2-factor authentication.
• Must be an ESRI business partner.

Comparison of the proposed price with prices found reasonable on previous purchases. Current price List. Comparison with similar items in a related industry. Comparison to IGCE. Contracting Officer's personal knowledge.

SCOPE OF WORK
Jackson County Schools is seeking proposals for a cloud-hosted, turnkey VOIP phone system. This will replace an existing on-premise Mitel system.

Current Environment
The current Mitel infrastructure has:
1. 1,370 desk phones and extensions
2. 228 DID numbers
3. No POTS or analog lines
4. Approximately 35 ATAs
5. All WAN links between sites are 10Gb
6. Current Internet bandwidth is 9.4Gb
7. All phones are connected to Meraki or Aruba switches and utilize POE

General Requirements
Proposed solutions should include:
1. Porting the district's current DID phone numbers to a new hosting provider
2. 1,370 new phones, equivalent to Mitel 6930 or Yealink T-44U
3. Phone options for hearing-impaired users
4. No off-net, metered, or otherwise limited extensions
5. Unlimited mobile app access for all users; if this is an advanced feature, please clearly state if there is a licensing limitation on how many users can access the mobile app or advanced features
6. Services and routing for E-911
7. A minimum of 20 auto attendants
8. A minimum of three 60 minute live training sessions
9. Centralized web management
10. Options for SSO integration
11. Options for reprogramming 35 existing ATAs, or costs for replacing them
12. All required setup, implementation, activation, and recurring service fees
13. Any limitations on supporting future growth and expansion of the proposed system

Overview

Tennessee Board of Regents (hereinafter Institution, TBR, or System) is soliciting proposals from qualified proposers for the procurement of a Noncredit Student Information System. The system will be used to process noncredit registrations for both open enrollment and customized corporate programs. This system must include registrations, invoices/billing, web payments for programs, customized templates for customer response, course schedules for web display and reporting. System must include a customizable contact hour report for institution reporting to THEC (grouped by type/category), contact hours (number registered * course hours assigned) with allowance for attendance hours if required, SIC / Industry codes (16 cluster codes to be supplied) and companies served (listed by name). Institutions may be required to produce weekly, monthly, quarterly and annual financial profit/loss at course level, section level and over profitability. Each course must include the ability to track direct and/or indirect costs associated with course administration. For forecasting financial data, the division produces reports based upon forecasted headcount or corporate sales. In addition, system must provide Google Analytics type data for student demographics and other promotional/marketing features.

Problem Statement: The United States Department of Commerce - has the need of a comprehensive commercial retirement benefits calculator platform for up to 45,000 unique employee profiles that is web-based with capabilities to obtain data from the National Finance Center or any third party to accurately compute various complex estimates needed in assisting employees with their retirement decisions. The purpose of the calculator is to electronically calculate various types of employee retirement benefits, retirement case management, and provide metrics/reports on benefits under the Civil Service Retirement System (CSRS), Civil Service Retirement System Offset (CSRS-Offset), Federal Employees Retirement System (FERS/FERS-RAE/FERS-FRAE), the Federal Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) and the Social Security Administration (SSA) program. The software must provide pre-populated forms, provide financial/retirement planning, provide retirement and benefits educational tools via videos, downloadable documents or other media. The software will include internal tools for TSP calculations, SSA calculations and for HR Specialist to make retirement coverage determinations and Federal Erroneous Retirement Coverage Corrections Act (FERCCA) determinations. HR Specialists must be able to view a Federal employee's record to assist employees inquiring about their Federal retirement and benefits. The calculator must be customizable to direct employees to a third party contactor. Additionally, the application generates approved versions of forms required for processing retirements and benefits that meet standards and guidance set forth by the Office of Personnel Management (OPM), Department of Labor - Office of Workers' Compensation Program, Federal Employee Group Life Insurance (FEGLI), Thrift Savings Plan, National Finance Center (NFC) and Internal Revenue Service (IRS). Affordability: ~$3M Over Five-Years

Automatically integrate and transfer required data for security threat assessments and Criminal History Records Checks (CHRC). Automate TSA mandated badge audits. Automate notifications to badge holders and signatories for badge expirations, renewal applications, and any required training.

B.2 STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW)

1. Contract Title
TrackCore Electronic Tissue Tracking System

2. Background
The Detroit VA Medical Center will need to track and manage biological and non-biological implant inventory using the TrackCore Implant and Tissue Tracking System effectively and efficiently. Reviewed by Joint Commission Resources, the system assists the facility in meeting regulatory agency and VA requirements related to implants. These requirements include the ability to bidirectionally track all implants/products from donor to facility to patient and back, per Joint Commission standards. System users can quickly and accurately pull implant information related to recalls, addressing stocked inventory as well as patients affected by recalled implants. The program reports, in real-time, current stock levels, par levels, alerts for expiring products, and cost, among other examples. The system maintains FDA/AATB certificates and IFU's for each vendor with the option to upload other documents.

3. Place of Performance
Detroit VA Medical Center
4646 John R. Street
Detroit, MI 48201

4. Period of Performance
Period of performance is from the award date through 5 years. The Contractor shall provide deliveries and maintenance under this contract during normal VA working hours (8:00 a.m. – 3:00 p.m.), Monday through Friday unless otherwise approved by the Contracting Officer.

The City of Shrewsbury seeks to implement a new Municipal Financial Software System that will enhance efficiency, and support process improvement and fiscal transparency. The city's primary interest is in moving toward a digital system which automates service delivery to the greatest extent practical, while ensuring compliance with Missouri laws, auditing requirements, and best practices. It would also be beneficial to have on-line services with self-service choices.

Software Qualifications
Software must be compatible with Microsoft 365. It should be intuitive, modern, and easily navigable. It must include fully customizable reports and the ability to export/import data in formats compatible with Microsoft Excel without extensive reformatting. Adding scanned electronic documents to various records across entire system (i.e., vendor files, accounts payables transactions, journal entries, etc.). Allow multiple users to work in the same module simultaneously, generating reports against the same data at the same time. Full integrations between modules, including single entry of information (i.e., using information across multiple modules rather than requiring duplicate entry, such as vendor purchase information automatically flowing in fixed asset record). Support for unlimited funds, policies, payments, journal entries, etc. It should either offer or be able to integrate with a client-driven on-line payment platform. All while having an audit trail/tracking of changes to records. The city also prioritizes ongoing support, training, and resources for users, and a skilled implementation process.

The city will entertain server-based, cloud-based, and hosted software and service proposals.

A. PROJECT BACKGROUND:
The Township of East Brunswick ("Township") wishes to purchase a solution that provides a secure cloud fax and emails for the Township's IT Department.
Quotes must be received by the Township's Designated Contact Person, via email, mail, or hand delivery, by 11:00 a.m. prevailing time on April 8, 2026. Quotes received after 11:00 a.m. on the Deadline Date will not be considered.

B. SCOPE OF WORK:
The Township is issuing this request for quotation via a fair and open process in accordance with N.J.S.A. 19:44A-20.4 et seq.
The Township seeks to procure the following services:
Cloud-Based Fax Services
The Township seeks to procure a secure cloud fax platform that eliminates the need for analog lines and fax hardware. The services should enable seamless, compliant faxing, designed for regulated industries, ensure the highest level of privacy, audit readiness, and global data sovereignty.
Email Platform Services
The Township seeks to procure a secure file exchange email platform designed for safe, encrypted sharing of sensitive documents and information and the transmittance of large files. The platform should ensure that sensitive information remains encrypted and protected from unauthorized access.
The solution must be able to handle 1 terabit of files.
