PROJECT OVERVIEW
Unified School District No. 500, Wyandotte County, Kansas, c/k/a Kansas City Kansas Public Schools (KCKPS), is seeking proposals from qualified firms to provide a districtwide data dashboard solution that integrates instructional, operational, and human capital data into a single, secure, and user-friendly platform. The selected solution will serve as a centralized source of truth for District data, enabling district leaders, school administrators, and instructional staff to efficiently access timely, accurate, and actionable information to support decision-making, accountability, and continuous improvement. The solution must integrate with KCKPS's existing enterprise and instructional systems and support robust data governance, role-based access, and equity-focused analysis.

This initiative is a key component of KCKPS's ongoing efforts to modernize data access, reduce manual reporting, increase transparency, and strengthen the District's ability to monitor academic performance, operational effectiveness, and student outcomes across schools and programs.

Project Highlights:
The Districtwide Data Dashboard Solution will:
• Provide a single, integrated platform that consolidates data from multiple District systems, reducing reliance on manual data aggregation and siloed reporting.
• Support instructional decision-making through configurable dashboards for district leaders, principals, teachers, and support staff.
• Enable equity-focused analysis by making subgroup data visible, comparable, and easy to analyze across schools, programs, and student populations.
• Deliver secure, role-based access to data with strong privacy, FERPA compliance, and data governance controls.
• Support longitudinal and trend analysis, including historical data and early warning indicators.
• Improve operational and human capital insights, including staffing, attendance, budget alignment, and workforce trends.
• Provide a scalable, sustainable solution that can evolve with District needs and support future data sources and reporting requirements.

I. BACKGROUND
The Washington Township School District is a suburban school district located in Gloucester County, NJ. The District serves approximately 7,700 students. The District has approximately 1400 employees, of which approximately 900 are full and/or part-time certified teaching positions. The District's current annual budget is in the amount of $150,000,000.
The District operates the following schools: 2 Early Childhood Center, 6 elementary schools, 3 middle schools and 1 high school. The District serves approximately 1,400 children with special needs. The District operates a Central Administration Building as well as an Operations Building.
II. SCOPE OF SERVICES
The Washington Township Board of Education is seeking priced proposals for an online data management, analytics, and assessment system for students in grades PreK-12, along with accompanying Professional Development Staff Training for the 2026-2027 school year. Services to be provided shall include the following:
A. Assessment and Data Dashboard
1. Warehouse all assessment data from any platform.
2. Create and administer assessments across all grades and subjects.
3. Collect results in customizable templates.

B. RTI/NJTSS and I&RS Software
1. Leverage warehoused multiple-measures and custom performance criteria to auto-tier students for NJTSS/RTI, determine programmatic eligibility, and roster students.
2. Set measurable goals, collect data to monitor progress, quantify outcomes, and evaluate effectiveness.

IX. EVALUATION AND AWARD OF CONTRACT

A. Evaluation Criteria: Proposals shall be evaluated using the following criteria.

Criteria:
1. Functional Fit & Requirements Compliance
a. Core SIS Features (gradebook, portals, mobile apps, attendance, discipline, scheduling, registration) – 10 points.
b. Data & Reporting (ad-hoc reporting, custom fields, transcript history, SQL access) – 6 points.
c. Interoperability (Ed-Fi, ClassLink, Canvas, Azure AD compliance) – 7 points.
d. Security & Hosting (SOC 2, MFA, encryption, RBAC, hosting SLA) – 7 points.
e. Virginia Reporting (VDOE compliance updates) – 5 points.
2. Past Performance & Experience
a. References and Virginia Experience (quality of references and experience working with VA divisions and VDOE reporting) – 13 points.
b. Company Stability (financial and organizational stability) – 3 points.
c. Security Track Record (documented breach history and maturity) – 3 points.
3. Cost (Total Cost of Ownership)
a. Five-Year Total Cost of Ownership (implementation, licensing, hosting, training, support) – 15 points.
b. Cost Transparency (clarity and completeness of pricing details) – 5 points.
c. Cost Reasonableness (comparison to industry norms) - 5 points.
4. Implementation & Training Plan
a. Project Schedule (feasibility of meeting 2026-27 go-live date) – 5 points.
b. Data Migration (mapping, test cycles, transcript/historical data migration) – 4 points.
c. Training Plan (training quantity and quality for all roles) - 3 points.
d. Support Model (post-go-live support and SLAs) - 3 points.
5. Certified locally owned business enterprise (LBE) – 2 points.
6. Certified minority business enterprise (MBE) – 2 points.
7. Certified women business enterprise (WBE) – 2 points.
Total Points 100

III. SCOPE OF SERVICES AND FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS

Offerors must provide a detailed response to each requirement below, indicating if the feature/function is "Native/Included," "Third-Party Integration," or "Not Available" (Attachment A).

A. Core Functional Modules
1. Teacher & Student Portals: The system must provide a robust gradebook and portal interface for teachers and students (comparable in functionality and user experience to "TeacherVUE/StudentVUE/ParentVUE" or functional equivalent).
2. Mobile Accessibility: Dedicated iOS and Android mobile applications for Parents, Students, Teachers, and Administrators with push notification capabilities.
3. Real-Time Grading: Instant synchronization between teacher gradebooks and student/parent portals.
4. Attendance & Discipline: Comprehensive attendance tracking, including Kiosk late check-in functionality for front offices.
5. Mass Scheduling: Automated and manual master scheduling tools capable of handling complex district logic.
6. Online Registration: A paperless, secure, and mobile-friendly online registration and enrollment system for new and returning students.
7. Training: Offeror must provide a "Distance/Training portal" providing on-demand video tutorials, manuals, and certification paths for staff.

B. Operations & Safety Integration
1. Hall Pass Integration: Digital hall pass management or seamless integration with leading electronic hall pass systems.
2. Visitor Management: Integrated Visitor Check-in module or verified compatibility with standard visitor management hardware/software.
3. Incident Response: Vendor must guarantee a 24-hour response time to critical incidents (system outages, security breaches, or data corruption).
4. Badges: Use of student badges with attendance, events, hall pass, and other applicable systems.

3 SCOPE OF WORK
Palace Transit seeks to procure a comprehensive, reliable dispatching software system to support and enhance the coordination, scheduling, and operational management of public transit services. This procurement is for a 5-year contract with the option to renew for additional terms following the initial contract period.

3.1 OBJECTIVES

Improve Operational Efficiency
Implement a unified dispatching system that streamlines scheduling, routing, and dispatch functions across both PT, reducing manual processes and enhancing service delivery.

Enhance Real-Time Coordination
Enable real-time vehicle tracking, driver communication, and service monitoring to support timely adjustments and improve responsiveness during service operations.

Support Multi-Agency Functionality
Deploy a scalable software solution capable of managing both PT operations individually or in combination, while maintaining secure, role-based data access and administrative controls.

Enhance Rider Experience
Provide riders with self-service tools for trip booking, tracking, fare management, and notifications through a user-friendly web portal or mobile interface.

Enable Data-Driven Decision Making
Offer robust reporting, analytics, and performance metric tools to support compliance, operational evaluation, and strategic planning.

Ensure System Integration and Compatibility
Integrate with existing systems (e.g., accounting, NTD reporting, mapping) and utilize familiar platforms (e.g., Google Maps, Bing Maps) for seamless deployment and operation.

Support Long-Term Growth and Adaptability
Establish a flexible and upgradeable platform that can accommodate future expansion, changes in service models, and evolving passenger needs.

Provide Comprehensive Training and Support
Deliver initial and ongoing training resources, system documentation, and 24/7 technical support to ensure successful adoption and continued system reliability.

Decision to Use the RFP Process
Pursuant to Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 30B, Section 6, the Town of Easton is utilizing a Request for Proposals (RFP) process for the procurement of an Integrated Financial Accounting Software System.

This procurement involves complex and integrated services that cannot be evaluated solely on the basis of price. The system must support governmental fund accounting, comply with applicable GASB and regulatory requirements, integrate with existing municipal systems, ensure data security, and provide implementation, training, and ongoing technical support.

Selection of a vendor requires evaluation of qualitative factors including technical capabilities, system functionality, vendor experience, implementation approach, cybersecurity safeguards, references, and long-term support capacity. These criteria necessitate a comparative evaluation process to determine the most advantageous proposal, taking into consideration both price and non-price factors.

Accordingly, the RFP process under Chapter 30B, Section 6 is appropriate and in the best interest of the municipality to ensure a fair, competitive, and value-based procurement.

The Town intends to select the most advantageous proposal, taking into consideration technical merit, experience, and price.

PURPOSE
The purpose of this Request for Proposals is to solicit interest from qualified firms to provide an inmate management system to the detention center.

INMATE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM SPECIFICATIONS
The Campbell County Detention Center, referred to as "CCDC", is requesting proposals from providers of inmate identification, security rounds, and activity logging. CCDC is particularly interested in proposals that include the use of technology in automating key operational workflows, tasks, and correctional reporting requirements as mandated by state corrections standards. This includes, but not limited to, tracking inmate movements and activities, logging cell checks, security checks, headcounts, inmate facial recognition software, commissary pass and other logs generated by CCDC for the purpose of tracking inmate interaction.

The Inmate Management System must be able to track inmate movements via passive, electronic means and logging of cell checks and security checks via fixed electronic scannable tags. All electronic log entries must record inmate name, inmate jacket number, deputy identification, date and time of entries. Electronically captured data cannot be edited, deleted, or altered in any way.

The Inmate Management System must: Interface with Jail Tracker, CCDC's existing jail management system; Use of a ruggedized handheld device with capabilities of sustaining a minimum of a 12 hour shift of average usage before recharging battery and have the ability to retrieve reports of inmate activity.

CCDC houses a daily population range of 400 to 500 inmates and maximum population of approximately 656 inmates.

APPENDIX B – SECTION M: Evaluation Criteria

Proposals will be evaluated on multiple factors to ensure the best overall value for the government. As part of this assessment, the government will consider the offeror's depth and relevance of experience with Microsoft Defender and Microsoft Purview in a government M365 E5/G5 environment. This experience will be evaluated in conjunction with other technical, management, and performance considerations and will not serve as the sole basis for determination:
• Demonstrated success in securing PII and sensitive agency information
• Ability to deploy, configure, and tune Purview DLP across workloads
• Proficiency in threat detection, incident response, and endpoint protection
• Understanding of GCC-specific considerations and limitations

Higher ratings where candidates demonstrate advanced certifications, extensive hands-on experience, or documented success implementing multi-layered security architectures using Defender, Purview, and complementary tools.

D.2 Evaluator Scoring Rubric (Illustrative Weights)
Criterion Weight Indicators of Strength
Purview DLP across workloads 25% — Intent→config mapping; SITs+EDM; monthly KPIs; exfil tests; tuning cycles
Defender stack + Sentinel 20% — Runbooks, triage workflows, SIEM integration use cases
Insider Risk & Adaptive Protection 15% — IRM policies; Adaptive Protection scoping; rollout notes
GCC/GCC High/DoD awareness & licensing 15% — Clear feature differences, compliance handling for CUI/PII
Email exfil protection 10% — Subject/body/attachments; domain scoping; Policy Tips/wait-on-send
Endpoint & Browser/Domain controls 10% — Copy/paste/print/USB restrictions; sensitive domain groups; approved browsers
Documentation & Knowledge Transfer 5% — Playbooks, naming conventions, exception handling, user education

Performance Work Statement
AFLCMC Engineering Directorate (EN-EZ)
Collateral Conference Room 162
1.0 Background
The U.S. Government requires a technology upgrade for the conference room located at conference room 162 located in Building 28, Area B, WPAFB, OH The purpose is to integrate a modern Unified Communications (UC) capability using Microsoft Teams into a Collateral Secret SIPRNET environment. The room contains existing VTC equipment that is outdated and scheduled for decommissioning but will remain in place temporarily. The new solution must be integrated with certain remaining components while replacing those that are incompatible.
2.0 Scope
The contractor shall provide and install a complete, turn-key solution to enable MS Teams functionality within the designated conference room. The work includes providing and installing a new camera, microphone system, and control system processor, and integrating these components with the existing displays and control interface. The final system must be capable of dual operation, allowing users to select either the new MS Teams capability or the legacy VTC system until it is decommissioned. The solution must be engineered to function independently after the legacy system is removed without requiring rework.

3.1 BACKGROUND
Morgan State University operates independently and is not linked to any human resources firms, subsidiary entities, or external consultants in the procurement of this software.
Morgan State University seeks to modernize its human resources capabilities by implementing a comprehensive Human Resources Management System (HRMS). The University aims to adopt a unified HRMS that supports the full spectrum of HR activities, including recruitment, onboarding, performance management, compensation management, learning management, employee relations case management, workforce planning, document management, compliance reporting, employee self-service functionalities, and manager self-service functionalities.
There will be approximately 25 HR team members who will require elevated access to manage their functions. The university has approximately 2400 total employees who would be customer end users.
The university plans to award a single award encompassing both the software and its implementation services; separate sourcing for implementation services will not be used. The procurement specialist will need to secure multiple approvals prior to finalizing a contract award, including necessary endorsements from internal stakeholders and, where pertinent, the Board of Public Works (BPW) if the contract value surpasses a defined threshold.

Competitive Contracting
FINANCIAL SOFTWARE INTEGRATED WITH HUMAN RESOURCES AND PAYROLL
Specifications
The system/programs shall be fully integrated and meet the requirements established and in full compliance as an enterprise resource planning system (ERP) in accordance with NJ.A.C. 6A:23A-6.7. The system/programs must be user friendly and intuitive, eliminate or reduce existing manual processes, produce reports in compliance with requirements of the State of New Jersey Department of Education and ad hoc queries quickly and easily. The system/programs must provide secure access to proprietary information in accordance with permissions granted, be scalable and customizable.
I. System/Multi-User Capability/Security
a. The application must be cloud based.
b. Provider's software must have been in use ten plus years in various size New Jersey school districts.
c. Application must run on both PC's and Mac's.
d. Data transmissions must be encrypted utilizing SSL.
e. The system should be scalable.
f. The system/programs must include document imaging links and ability to drill down for detail on any previewed report.
g. The programs must include the option for electronic approvals and electronic check signatures, and the proposal shall stipulate the internal security established by the system. An accredited and nationally recognized e-signature authority/provider should be used and identified.

SECTION M - EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Technical Evaluation Criteria

The following criteria shall be utilized in evaluation of the technical quote. Each of the following criteria will be evaluated on a Pass/Fail basis in accordance with the criteria descriptions included in Section M. In order to be eligible for award, your quote should include adequate detail and information for the Government evaluators to be able to rate each criteria as satisfactory (Pass). A rating of "Fail" in any one of these three criteria will render the quote ineligible for award until the deficiencies are corrected.

Technical evaluations for this RFQ are as follows: (limited to 20 pages)

1. Technical Capability:

The Offeror's explanation shall include name of the digital evaluation platform and an analysis of the platform task requirements to indicate their understanding of the requirement.

The above listed criteria are listed in order of descending importance and, when combined, the technical factors are significantly more important than price.

2. Scope of Work.
The scope of work includes the procurement, implementation, training and support of an advanced weapons detection system designed to enhance campus safety through real-time video analytics and seamless integration with the existing security infrastructure.

1. Accuracy
a. Accurately identifying a weapon when it is present. Leading systems often achieve high accuracy rates, such as 95–99%, for clearly visible weapons.
b. Minimizing false alarms when an object is not a weapon.
c. Availability of visible confidence level shown for each alarm
d. Reliable long-range operation, detecting weapons from a distance, dependent on camera resolution.
2. Speed
a. Low Latency- processing video almost instantaneously
b. High frame rate- to avoid missing threats
3. Robustness- perform reliably under challenging conditions
a. Environmental resilience- various lighting conditions low-light, shadows and weather
b. Visual Clutter- campus atmosphere heavy traffic at times
4. Weapon recognition capability- The system should be able to identify a wide range of weapons, including handguns, rifles, shotguns, and knives.
5. Integration with existing systems
a. Camera compatibility: Working with standard Internet Protocol (IP) cameras of multiple manufacturers and Genetec (VMS).
b. Multi-camera support: Monitoring and analyzing feeds from an entire network of cameras.

The Abrams Ground Vehicle Platform is adopting Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) to ensure the timing, accuracy, and reliability of critical data messages. Onboard computing devices are equipped with proprietary TSN configuration and testing software (e.g., GE Aerospace's Chronos & Helios) and are networked with TSN switches. To ensure successful integration and compliance with stringent performance requirements, government engineers require specialized training on these specific systems and standards.

C.2 Objective
The objective of this training is to provide Abrams program engineers with the in-depth knowledge and practical, hands-on skills required to effectively configure, manage, analyze, and test TSN networks on the Abrams Ground Vehicle Platform.

Evaluation Factor 1: Small Business Participation
The Government will evaluate the offeror's Small Business Participation submission on an "Acceptable" or "Unacceptable" basis. The criteria for an "Acceptable" rating differ for "Other Than Small Business" and "Small Business" offerors.

For the purposes of all calculations under this factor, offerors shall use $500M for the "Total Contract Value" (TCV). This TCV is for evaluation standardization only and does not represent a guarantee or ceiling.

Table 2: MQRs for this Requirement
SB 23% of the total acquisition value
SDB 5% of the total acquisition value
WOSB 5% of the total acquisition value
HUBZone 3% of the total acquisition value
SDVOSB 5% of the total acquisition value

Note: Double counting is permitted. A participation plan that reflects the required percentage of the acquisition value for any of the specific small business categories (SDB, WOSB, HUBZone, SDVOSB) will also count towards the overall 23% small business goal. The Government must be able to verify that each percentage is based on the total evaluated price.

Large business offerors are required to submit a formal Small Business Subcontracting Plan. This plan will be evaluated to determine the extent to which the offeror commits to utilizing small businesses. The plan must contain commitments, goals, and dollars that meet or exceed the Minimum Quantitative Requirements (MQRs) listed in Table 2, or provide an acceptable, detailed rationale as to why an MQR cannot be met.

The mission of the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) Research Directorate is to deliver future Geospatial-Intelligence (GEOINT) capabilities to users for operational impact. NGA Research supports the National Security Strategy by solving hard defense and intelligence problems for the Intelligence Community and Department of Defense. In support of this mission, NGA Research promotes research initiatives across a broad spectrum of science and engineering disciplines, and transforms these initiatives into disruptive GEOINT capabilities in support of U.S. national security. The Boosting Innovative GEOINT-Science and Technology (BIG-ST) Broad Agency Announcement (BAA) invites proposers to submit innovative concepts to address hard GEOINT problems that align to one or more of the following technical domains: (1) Foundational GEOINT, (2) Advanced Phenomenologies, and (3) Analytic Technologies. Each of these domains is described below and includes a list of example research topics that highlight several (but not all) potential areas of interest. Throughout the open period of this BAA, posted Topic Calls will specify areas associated with these technical domains. Each posted Topic Call will provide specific timelines for abstract and proposal submission. Proposers are responsible for maintaining awareness of these Topic Calls and associated timelines for submissions. No submissions shall be accepted to the general solicitation; abstracts and proposals will only be reviewed in response to Topic Calls.

2.3.2 Quality Control Plan (QCP)
2.3.2.1 The Contractor shall develop and maintain an effective quality control program to ensure services are performed IAW this PWS, applicable laws and regulations, and best commercial practices. The Contractor shall develop and implement procedures to identify, prevent, and ensure non-recurrence of defective services with special emphasis placed on those services listed in this PWS. The Contractor's quality control program is the means by which it assures itself that the work complies with the requirement of the contract. The Contractor shall periodically update the QCP as changes in program processes occur. The draft QCP is due NLT 10 working days after project start. The final QCP is due NLT 30 working days after project start. At a minimum, the QCP shall be reviewed and updated once a year. The Contractor's QCP shall describe, at a minimum, the following:
a. Approach and procedures for communicating with the Government, resolving issues, and identifying potential program improvements for Government consideration.
b. Quality control methodology performance expectations and objectives including benchmark metrics and measures.
c. Detailed description of the Contractor's internal quality review process including the individual(s) responsible, how frequently quality reviews occur, the method(s) that shall be utilized, and what services/products/capabilities shall be reviewed by the Contractor.

SECTION 6 - ITERATIVE PROTOTYPING

Prototype awards made under this CSO will allow for an iterative prototyping process. An iterative prototyping process will allow the Government to modify, by mutual agreement, the work in a prototype project to allow the adaptation and modification of the technology being prototyped to meet additional unique and discrete purposes/mission sets.

SECTION 7 – FOLLOW-ON PRODUCTION

Upon successful completion of any prototype project under the OTA, the Government and Company may negotiate a follow-on production contract or agreement without further competition. Any concept/technology/solution successfully proven through a Prototype OTA can be transitioned to production.

SECTION 8 – SUCCESSFUL COMPLETION

A transaction for a prototype project is complete upon the written determination of the appropriate approving official (Agreement Officer) for the matter in question that efforts conducted under a Prototype OTA: (1) met the key technical goals of a project; (2) satisfied success metrics incorporated into the Prototype OTA; or (3) accomplished a particularly favorable or unexpected result that justifies the transition to production.

(a) The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation to the responsible offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government, price and other factors considered. The following factors shall be used to evaluate offers:

Quotes will be evaluated on the following factors:
1. Technical Approach
2. Experience
3. Past Performance

The Government will evaluate quotations using the comparative evaluation process where quotations will be compared to one another to determine which provides the best benefit to the Government. The Government reserves the right to consider a quotation other than the lowest price that provides additional benefit(s).

(b) Options. The Government will evaluate offers for award purposes by adding the total price for all options to the total price for the basic requirement. The Government may determine that an offer is unacceptable if the option prices are significantly unbalanced.

In addition, TAD-40 work performed utilizes the pricing mechanism and principles described at FAR Part 16, Types of Contracts.

3.1 Program Management
As part of the Deployment Approach (A003) and the Implementation Plan (A010), the contractor shall provide a program management framework to ensure deployment efforts are governed by consistent standards of quality, risk management, and fiscal discipline. This framework shall provide the Government with full transparency into project performance and ensure the contractor is accountable for all outcomes. The contractor shall successfully execute all work under this contract, including the necessary processes and tools to effectively manage cost, schedule, and performance/resources.

The contractor shall designate a Program Manager (PM) who will serve as the Government's primary point of contact. The PM must be authorized to act on behalf of the contractor for all matters related to this contract and its subsequent task orders. The PM is responsible for the overall management of contract performance, including resource allocation, risk mitigation, and ensuring clear communication with the Government's Contracting Officer's Representative (COR).

• Staffing Plan: The contractor shall develop, maintain, and deliver a Staffing Plan (D003) that details the roles and labor categories required to successfully execute all tasks specified in the task order.

The Department anticipates that the user base, supported organizations, and operational footprint may expand over the period of performance. While the scope and timing of any such expansion cannot be precisely defined at this time, offerors should be aware that future requirements may include support for periods of increased operational tempo and additional participating entities consistent with evolving mission needs.

5.4.6 Data Integration Requirements

Vendors must describe their integration methodology, API architecture, and data security protocols for connecting to UCO's internal systems. Vendors must specify for each whether integration is native/out-of-the-box or requires custom development. The platform must integrate with the following systems. Alternative approaches to direct APIs is a viable option where data subsets of these systems are provided to give the AI context or access to information needed.

• Banner (Student Information System): Enrollment status, academic records, demographic data, degree audit data, and financial aid information.
  - Banner SIS — Integration Note: UCO is currently completing a migration from on-premises Banner to Banner Managed Cloud (BMC). This transition is expected to be complete by March 2026, at which point UCO will have access to Ellucian Ethos Integration Services and the full Ethos API ecosystem. Vendors should architect their Banner integration to leverage Ethos APIs as the primary integration pathway.
• Brightspace (Learning Management System): Academic progress and course enrollment data to support advising capabilities.
• Slate (Admissions CRM): Lead capture for prospective students, application status, communication history, and enrollment funnel data
• Career Services Platforms: Job posting feeds, employer relationship data, and internship/placement records
• Financial Aid Systems: Connecting queries to Aid eligibility, application process, and scholarship data.
• UCO.edu Website: Primary web-based student engagement layer

3.0 TERMS OF THE CONTRACT
3.1 Timeline
The customer intends to award based on deliverables. The Contractor must provide comprehensive, on-site and remote assistance to establish a secure cardholder data environment, remediate vulnerabilities, and empower local personnel to maintain long-term PCI compliance. Performance begins upon award of the contract and concludes 12 months after contract award with additional option years considered.

Period of Performance: 12 months, with option years as required.

3.2 Contractor Qualifications
A minimum of five (5) years of hands-on experience is required. AFSVC requires a technical support contractor with expertise in cybersecurity and a track record of successfully supporting and guiding IT teams in securing and monitoring networks and expanding their knowledge base. The ideal candidate must possess a strong background in network security and cybersecurity. Prior experience working with the Federal Government or DoW is highly desirable.

The Contractor must provide the experience necessary to complete the tasks outlined in the SOO. The Contractor must utilize established policies, standards, and sound management principles in the performance of the SOO requirements and deliverables.

The place of performance is to be at the direction and discretion of the contractor to fulfill the requirements of this contract in an acceptable manner.

7.1. Travel.

The Government anticipates that both local and non-local domestic travel will be required for certain positions during the term of this IDIQ to attend workshops and/or other meetings as authorized by the COR. The Contractor shall identify, forecast, and manage travel requests. All travel must be approved by the COR at least 10 business days in advance of the travel requirement. The Contractor shall maintain a travel log of all approved trips and include all estimated/vouchered costs, which may be reviewed by the COR as needed. During performance, travel costs are reimbursed in accordance with Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 31.205-46 and Federal Travel Regulations, subject to the COR's approval. Any travel conducted without prior coordination with the COR will be done at the Contractor's own risk and expense.

ARTICLE I:   SCOPE OF THE AGREEMENT

A.	Background

THIS PARAGRAPH(S) DESCRIBES THE VISION OF THE PROGRAM AND SHOULD ANSWER THE FOLLOWING QUESTIONS:  

WHAT IS THE PURPOSE OF THE AGREEMENT?  

WHAT IS THE CURRENT TECHNOLOGICAL SITUATION?  

WHAT MAKES THIS PROGRAM A "CRITICAL TECHNOLOGY" EFFORT?  

WHY IS THE CURRENT TECHNOLOGY NOT SUFFICIENT?  

WHY IS IT NECESSARY FOR THE GOVERNMENT TO DEVELOP THE PROTOTYPE AND WHAT IS ITS POTENTIAL IMPACT ON THE MILITARY AND COMMERCIAL SECTOR?  

WHAT ARE THE ISSUES OF PARTICULAR IMPORTANCE TO DARPA?  

WHAT IS THE MARKET POTENTIAL? WHAT ARE THE COMMERCIALIZATION GOALS? 

IF THE PROGRAM IS SUCCESSFUL, THEN WHAT?  WHERE DO WE GO FROM HERE?  IF THIS COLLABORATION IS SUCCESSFUL, WHAT WILL WE HAVE ACCOMPLISHED?

C.	Scope

1.	This Agreement is an Other Transaction pursuant to 10 U.S.C. § 4022.  The principal purpose of this Agreement is to engage in a research and development program for the development of a (DESCRIBE PROTOTYPE).

2.	The Performer shall be responsible for performance of the work set forth in the Task Description Document (TDD) incorporated in this Agreement as Attachment 1.   The Performer shall submit or otherwise provide all documentation required by Attachment 2, Report Requirements.

3.	This Agreement can best be described as an expenditure-based approach with milestones and associated fees.  The Performer shall be paid for each milestone accomplished in accordance with the Schedule of Milestones and Payments set forth in Attachment 3 and the procedures of Article V.

On November 30, 2020, the Kennedy Space Center (KSC) Office of Procurement (OP) Commercial Space Office awarded a GSA OASIS CPFF TO to MEI Technologies, Inc. (MEIT). MEIT has since restructured as Aegis Aerospace, Inc. (Aegis). The CPFF TO included a 10-month base period, with three 12-month option periods, ending September 30, 2024. While the Aegis OASIS master contract term expired December 19, 2024, TO Period of Performance (POPs) may extend for up to five years from the master contract term expiration date. In July 2024, a 1-year option year was added extending the period of performance through September 30, 2025. In July 2025, an additional two-month option period (October 1, 2025 – November 29, 2025) was added to the contract to extend the period of performance to a full five years. The justification for an exception to competition adding the 2-month option period inaccurately stated that a second option extending the contract from November 30, 2025, to May 29, 2026, was being added. No additional option period was added. The TO included FAR 52.217-8, Option to Extend Services, however, pricing was not evaluated at the time of award. As a result, the agency obtained pricing for the potential 6-month period that would be used if the FAR 52.217-8, Option to Extend Services was exercised.

5.4.1 Reasonableness (Applies to all CLINs and Attachment 3 Rate Schedule)
The Offeror's proposal will be assessed for price reasonableness, which is an assessment of whether the evaluated price is too high. The price is reasonable if, in its nature and amount, it does not exceed that amount which would be paid by a prudent person in the conduct of competitive business (FAR 31.201-3). In conducting the reasonableness analysis, the Government will use one or more price analysis techniques including, but not limited to, those described in FAR 15.404-1.
A determination of an "unreasonable" price will render the proposal unawardable. In addition, proposals that have been assessed to be "unacceptable" in Technical Capability or Technical Risk are ineligible for award, and as a result, a reasonableness determination will not be made.

5.4.2 Cost Realism (Applies to Cost-Reimbursable/Cost Plus Award Fee Task Order CLINs)
The Offeror's proposal will be assessed for cost realism; the assessment will analyze whether the proposed price is too low. Cost realism analysis is the process of independently reviewing and evaluating specific elements of each offeror's proposed cost estimate to determine whether the estimated proposed cost elements are realistic for the work to be performed; reflect a clear understanding of the requirements; and are consistent with the unique methods of performance and materials described in the Offeror's technical proposal. (FAR 15.404-1(d)(1))

The upward cost adjustments will result in the Government's Most Probable Cost that will become part of the TEP calculation for best value consideration.

VENDOR SHORT-LIST EVALUATION CRITERIA
The RFP selection committee may choose to interview one or all of the short-listed Vendors. Both the original submitted proposals and the results of any Vendor interviews and professional references will be used to select the final Vendor for the project. The following criteria are among those that will be used to evaluate the Vendors on the short-list:
1. Proposed Solution Functionality and Technical Design:
a. This criteria pertains to system functionality and design, including the experience of parking customers, and the City as the parking owner and operator. Aspects of the parking experience include accepting payment, event parking, navigating the devices, equipment, conducting enforcement, and collecting and reviewing data.
2. Proposed Approach approach/methodology completing the scope of services such as:
a. Logic and convenience of the proposed approach and methodology relative to the city's needs. Compatibility of the proposed approach and methodology to city policies and practices in administering this type of public improvement project.
b. Grasp of project requirements and level of interest in the project.
c. Creativity and problem-solving ability.
3. Experience, Qualifications, and References
a. Experience and qualifications of all team members (including any subconsultants) executing similar projects for municipalities similar to Dubuque, including in size, geographic market, weather conditions, and customer/user profile.
b. Responsiveness and compatibility between the Vendor and City, including general attitude and ability to communicate and be a high-quality team player, ability of the Vendor to maintain a high level of direct interaction and communication with city staff, ability to listen, be flexible, and follow and/or implement direction and/or ideas or concepts.
c. A Track record of all the vendor team members working together.
4. Ongoing System Maintenance and Support.
5. Proposed schedule required to complete project.
6. Cost of the proposed project.

1.2 STEP TWO (2) – TRADE-OFF
The Step Two Trade-Off evaluation consists of the following factors:
FACTOR 1: TECHNICAL ACCEPTABILITY FACTOR 2: PAST PERFORMANCE FACTOR 3: PRICE

FACTOR 1: TECHNICAL ACCEPTABILITY
All compliant proposals will be evaluated by a panel of Space Radiation experts. This evaluation includes an assessment of the scientific and technical merit, the relevance to objectives stated in this RFQ, and ability of the proposed work to evaluate the effect of Radiation Quality and Galactic Cosmic Radiation Simulation on the Risk of Carcinogenesis in an Outbred Mouse Model (HS/Npt). Adjectival scores will be assigned for Technical Approach.

FACTOR 2: PAST PERFORMANCE
Quoter shall provide a maximum of three past performance references.
Quoters shall submit reference biosketches for their Key personnel. The biosketch must reference papers that show ability to conduct this work with similar nature, scope, and complexity to the effort described in this RFQ.

FACTOR 3: PRICE
Pricing data for proposals will be evaluated both by peer review for price reasonableness and by NASA program personnel for total price and comparison to available funds. In evaluating the price reasonableness of the proposals, reviewers will assess the proposed level of effort (i.e. FTE) and the proposed other direct costs (i.e. supplies, equipment, travel) are commensurate with those required to accomplish the goals described in the proposal.

2.2 Step Two (2) – TRADE-OFF
The trade-off factors below are listed in descending order of importance. Technical Acceptability is more important than Past Performance. Past Performance is more important than Price. Technical Acceptability and Past Performance, when combined, are significantly more important than Price. Price is not the most important evaluation factor, but its degree of importance will increase commensurably with the degree of equality among different Quoter's Technical and Past Performance quotes.

Process the files relying on procedures and checklists developed by the government. The procedures include resolving all new units, all edit and screening errors, and changes in administrative information such as firm name, address, and other identifying information.

Follow post-processing procedures, which include but are not limited to updating firm notes based on new information and changes to processing steps identified during processing, entering firm processing data metrics into the EDI website, updating the EDI website with any changes in firm administrative information.

Respond to any file or data issues that arise during processing and pre-processing using established procedures, contact respondents for incomplete or missing information, and answering questions from the states or government, making changes requested by states within the government-designated timeframes.

Write programs to reformat firm-supplied data into standard data formats, while developing and maintaining technical documentation and user manuals for any systems developed in-house.

The General Services Administration (GSA), Public Buildings Service, Region 4 (Southeast Sunbelt Region), is soliciting quotations for quarterly pressure washing and exterior surface cleaning services at the Charles R. The contractor shall furnish all labor, supervision, materials, cleaning agents (which must be biodegradable and compliant with EPA environmental standards), equipment, and transportation necessary to perform pressure washing of all exterior concrete sidewalks, building entrance areas, loading dock surfaces, parking garage ramps and stairwells, and the exterior plaza area. Work shall be performed quarterly (four times per year) during non-business hours (after 6:00 PM or on weekends) to minimize disruption to building tenants.

Quotations shall include a completed pricing schedule (Attachment A), proof of commercial general liability insurance ($1,000,000 minimum), and two references for similar work performed at federal or commercial facilities within the past three years.

(i) Basis for Contract Award. B. Evaluation Factors for Award

An offeror must receive a rating of 'Acceptable' for both Factor 1 and Factor 2 to be eligible for award. For offerors deemed eligible, the award decision will be based on a best-value tradeoff where Factor 3 is more important than Factor 4.

A rating of 'Low Confidence' reflects a high degree of risk but does not, in itself, make a proposal unawardable. The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation to the responsible Offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government, price and other factors considered.

Selection Process

The Defense Logistics Agency (DLA) Transportation Process Support requirement will utilize the Trade-off evaluation process where technical approach, key personnel, management and staffing plan, and past performance when combined are significantly more important than total price. The tradeoff process is appropriate when it is in the best interest of the government to consider award(s) to other than the lowest priced quoter or other than the highest technically rated quoter. This process permits trade-offs among cost or price and non-cost/price factors and allows the government to accept other than the lowest priced offer. The perceived benefits of the higher priced offer must merit the additional cost/price, and the rationale for trade-offs must be documented in the contract file. The government's objective is to obtain the highest quality services considered necessary to achieve the required objectives.

Relative importance. All non-price evaluation factors when combined are significantly more important than price. The non-price evaluation factors are of equal importance. As the non-price evaluation factors become more equal among quotes, the evaluated price becomes more important.

Basis for award(s). Quotes will first be evaluated on the basis of the non-price evaluation factors as defined below.

VII. Application Review Information
A. Evaluation Criteria
Conforming proposals will be evaluated using the following criteria, listed in descending order of importance. Evaluators will base their conclusions only on information contained in the proposal. It cannot be assumed that evaluators are acquainted with the firm or key individuals or any referenced experiments. Relevant supporting data such as journal articles, literature, including Government publications, etc., should be contained or referenced in the proposal and will count toward the page limit.

1. Overall Operational and Technical Merit
The proposed technical approach is innovative, feasible, achievable, and complete. Concepts shall specifically and clearly address the innovation proposed and the operational or technical basis of the claims. The proposed technical team shall reflect the expertise and experience to accomplish the proposed tasks. Task descriptions and associated technical elements provided are complete and in a logical sequence with all proposed deliverables clearly defined such that a final outcome that achieves the goal can be expected as a result of award.

Sufficient information demonstrating an executable course of research shall be provided for reviewers to determine whether it would enable capabilities beyond state-of-the-art or state-of-practice. The proposal identifies major risks and planned mitigation efforts are clearly defined and feasible. Barriers to implementation shall be discussed and potential mitigations for such barriers addressed.

2. Potential Contribution and Relevance to the SDA Mission
Proposals will also be assessed against the SDA mission and the focus on PWSA enhancements that benefit the warfighter. SDA is not interested in approaches or technologies that are comparable to the current state-of-practice, or duplicative of on-going efforts. Proposals will also be evaluated on the potential for commercial (Government or private sector) application and the benefits expected to accrue from resulting commercialization or commoditization.

SECTION 1 – INTRODUCTION

1.1 Background and Authority

The Naval Undersea Warfare Center (NUWC) is responsible for delivering undersea warfare (USW) capability that enables decisive warfighting advantage to the Fleet. NUWC is expanding Tactical Oceanography (TAC-O) prototyping efforts that include the development of new onboard and offboard environmental sensors and devices that build operator situational awareness and information systems that enable operators to increase performance of existing systems through expert use of the environment.

NUWC utilizes prototype Other Transaction Agreements (OTA or Agreements) under the authority of 10 U.S.C. § 4022 to partner with nontraditional and traditional defense contractors and non-profit research institutions to carry out prototype projects that are directly relevant to enhancing the mission effectiveness of military personnel and the supporting platforms, systems, components, or materials proposed to be acquired or developed by the DoD, or the improvement of platforms, systems, components, or materials in use by the armed forces.

The Government intends to award up to $80,000,000 through prototype project awards that ultimately result from this CSO.

Per the Office of Management Budget (OMB), any cloud services that hold federal data must be authorized by the Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP). All Federal data must be stored on a FedRAMP authorized systems, and loss of FedRAMP authorization is equivalent to the inability to house federal data via a cloud service. FedRAMP authorization applies to all third parties and subcontractors that the vendor uses to store federal data. Proof of FedRAMP authorization must be provided, and the vendor must disclose where all data is stored. If any data is stored by a third party and/or subcontractor, the vendor must provide proof of FedRAMP authorization for these third parties and subcontractors. FedRAMP authorization must always be maintained by the vendor and all third parties and subcontractors the vendor uses to store federal data. All cryptographic modules and hardware security modules (HSMs) must be FIPS 140-2 certified. The vendor must provide proof of FIPS 140-2 certification via a NIST approved validator. The operating platform upon which the FIP 140-2 certification was obtain must be maintained.

SECOND STEP - Proposal Peer or Scientific Review Criteria: Proposals will be reviewed against the criteria listed below. The technical aspect, which is ranked as the first order of priority, shall be reviewed based on the following criteria that are of descending order of importance:

a) Technical:
(1) Unique and innovative approach proposed to accomplish the technical objectives. New and creative solutions and/or advances in knowledge, understanding, technology, and the state of the art.
(2) The offeror's understanding of the scope of the technical effort.
(3) Soundness of the offeror's technical approach including whether the proposal identifies major technical risks, clearly defines feasible mitigation efforts, and demonstrates related experience and qualifications of technical personnel.
(4) The potential to transition the research and development deliverables to future Government needs. Any proposed restriction on technical data or computer software will be considered.

b) Cost/Price: The cost evaluation includes the realism of the proposed cost. Cost/Price is a substantial factor, but ranked as the second order of priority. (If an offeror proposes the use of GFP other than any GFP identified in this BAA, and that proposed GFP provides the offeror an unfair competitive advantage, then FAR 45.202 requires rental equivalent be applied to the Cost Factor for evaluation purposes only).

Second Step Selection Categories: Based on the Peer or Scientific Review, proposals will be categorized as Selectable or Not Selectable (see definitions below). The selection of one or more sources for award will be based on the Peer or Scientific Review, as well as importance to agency programs and funding availability.
(1) Selectable: Proposals are recommended for acceptance if sufficient funding is available.
(2) Not Selectable: Even if sufficient funding existed, the proposal should not be funded.
No other criteria will be used.
Prior to award of a potentially successful offer, the Contracting Officer will make a determination regarding price reasonableness.

Solicitation Number: VA-248-25-Q-0197
Department of Veterans Affairs – Network Contracting Office 8
Posted Date: May 12, 2025
Response Deadline: May 30, 2025, 2:00 PM EST

I. OVERVIEW
The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), James A. Haley Veterans' Hospital, Tampa, FL, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for the provision and installation of an Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)-compliant wayfinding signage system within Building 100, floors 1 through 3. This solicitation is issued as a Request for Quotation (RFQ) under FAR Part 13.

II. NAICS CODE AND SIZE STANDARD
NAICS Code: 339950 – Sign Manufacturing
Size Standard: 500 employees
This procurement is set aside for Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Businesses (SDVOSB) in accordance with VAAR 819.7005.

III. BUDGET AND PRICING
The total available funding for this contract is $55,000.00. This represents the maximum contract value and includes all labor, materials, fabrication, installation, project management, and cleanup. Quotations that exceed the stated budget ceiling will not be considered for award.

IV. PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
All work shall be completed within 60 calendar days after receipt of the Notice to Proceed. Work shall be performed during normal business hours (7:00 AM – 4:30 PM, Monday through Friday), excluding Federal holidays. After-hours work may be authorized by the COR on a case-by-case basis.

V. DELIVERY AND ACCEPTANCE
Final acceptance shall be contingent upon a joint walk-through with the COR, Facility Safety Officer, and the contractor to verify conformance with VA Signage Design Guide specifications and ADA Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG).

5.3 Architecture Overview – Hub-and-Spoke Model

UCO will implement a Hub-and-Spoke AI architecture where shared, centralized infrastructure powers multiple student-facing AI applications. Vendors must demonstrate their ability to design, deliver, and support this model.

Component | Purpose | Implementation Phase
The Hub | Core AI platform, system integrations, security framework, analytics, and data layer shared across all spokes. Includes secure AI Platform Infrastructure, Data Integration Framework, ML templates, centralized Security & Privacy Framework, and Ethical AI Governance. | Phase 1
Spoke 0 — Sentinel | Safety and compliance layer monitoring all AI interactions for harmful content, crisis signals, policy violations, and PII. Provides real-time escalation to UCO counseling and campus police. | Phase 1
Spoke 1 — Admissions & Career | AI-driven prospective student engagement, application guidance, financial aid information, career-to-program matching, skills gap analysis, and Oklahoma labor market alignment. | Phase 1
Spoke 2 — AI Success Buddy | Proactive academic advising support for enrolled students — degree planning, at-risk student outreach, course sequencing, and seamless human advisor handoff. | Phase 2

The hub infrastructure must be designed to support a minimum of 1,000 concurrent users, achieve 99.5% or greater uptime in production, and maintain consistent response latency for AI-served queries.

B. Description of Opportunity:

DoW seeks technology solutions that incorporate multi-modal agile manufacturing to advance operational continuity for Contested Logistics using new manufacturing processes and/or emerging production technologies. Specifically, DoW is interested in pilot production cells capable of integrating elements such as digitized design, additive manufacturing, robotics, joining, and assembly to create a finished metal part from powdered metal feedstocks. Solutions should prioritize high product mix and rapid cycle times for parts production and sustainment. Solutions must clearly identify the technology they intend to demonstrate, where it would be demonstrated, and any technology development needed to ensure success, metrics of success. Solutions must include maximum part dimensions, material types and properties, suitability for part certification/qualification, and expected production rates and capacity for parts of at least 16 inches in any one dimension.

The DoW has established a target of a 24-month performance period and $15M in federal funding for this prototype. Proposals that exceed these parameters will be considered if accompanied by a compelling justification that details the added value, innovation, and rationale for the increased cost.

Basis for Contract Award. Award will be given to the responsible proposer whose proposal is determined to represent the overall best value to the Government using a best value tradeoff (BVTO) evaluation process. The offeror's technical proposal should include all information the offeror wants the Government to consider and evaluate regarding its ability to perform all required tasks and conform to all required terms and conditions. To be eligible for award, the offeror's proposal must sufficiently address all aspects of the PWS.

B. Evaluation Factors for Award
The Government will use the following factors to evaluate each proposal. Factors 1 and 2 are evaluated on an Acceptable/Unacceptable basis. An offeror must receive a rating of 'Acceptable' for both Factor 1 and Factor 2 to be eligible for award. For offerors deemed eligible, the award decision will be based on a best-value tradeoff where Factor 3 is more important than Factor 4.

Factor 4: Price
The Government will evaluate the final proposed price for reasonableness and completeness.

D. Price
1. The offeror's price proposal will be evaluated based upon the total evaluated price in the five base years, all five option years, and the option pricing for an additional six-month period under FAR 52.217-8.

The Government will not be performing any realism analysis to determine whether the offeror's overall price reflects a lack of understanding of the requirements or is otherwise too low.

C.4 Scope
Under this contract, the Contractor shall expand the scope and complexity of the data aggregation, anonymization, and preparation needed to enable future analyses. The contractor shall advance the level of maturity of the partnership and the trusted third-party role by deepening the level of collaboration between the partners, maturing the data-sharing platform to accelerate independent analyses for comparison, and increasing the number of participant organizations. As such, the work performed under this contract is non-severable. Specifically, the Contractor shall:
1) Expand and mature the breadth and depth of the partnership by:
a) Providing necessary governance and management to ensure success of the data trust and partnership.
b) Providing and leveraging highway safety expertise to facilitate novel research that can only be accomplished in a data trust.
c) Refining the data ingestion, anonymization, and analytical processes established to date.
d) Establishing the data architecture and governance to enable novel analyses.
2) Further develop the data analysis capabilities system by:
a) Incorporating additional federal, state, industry, etc. data sources.
b) Expanding the data trust and partnership to include additional members and sources of data.
3) Assist the government to maintain the partnership by:
a) Maintaining and updating the research roadmap.
b) Developing a sustainable data architecture and analysis.

C.5.1. General Requirements:
All contractor personnel providing AV/IT support services at MSCoE G6 supported facilities at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, shall meet the minimum qualifications and certification requirements outlined in this section. • Minimum of one (1) year of experience in AV/IT support or a related field
• CompTIA A+ certification or equivalent
• Basic knowledge of AV systems, including projectors, audio systems, and display panels

• Strong communication and customer service skills
C.5.3. Tier 2 Technician Qualifications:

• Certified Technology Specialist (CTS) certification preferred
• Advanced knowledge of AV control systems (e.g., Crestron, Extron, AMX)
• Advanced knowledge of IT infrastructure, including network switches, servers, and cybersecurity compliance
• Experience with VTC systems and collaboration platforms (e.g., Microsoft Teams, Zoom)

1.7. Ethical considerations

The biological computational capabilities being developed under this solicitation are intended to significantly advance the field of biocomputing but are still far removed from natural brains. However, the growing field of biological computation could raise ethical concerns. As such, proposals should include a component that highlights any key bioethics considerations that may be relevant to the specific approach being proposed. Additionally, performer teams will be required to periodically reassess/update their ethical consideration assessment throughout the program (e.g., as part of their end of Phase reporting).

1.8. T&E: Government Independent Test and Evaluation

The O-Circuit program will include an independent T&E component as part of the Government team. The T&E team (not being sought in this solicitation) will be responsible for developing the simulated game environment used for assessing BPU capabilities for TA1. They will also provide drone platforms that will be used for the end of Phase Capability Demonstrations to TA2 performers (making details of those platforms available to performers so that matching systems can be acquired if desired), develop a TA2 odorant testbed for assessing detection accuracy for both Phase 1 and Phase 2, and develop a drone navigation, odorant detection and gradient navigation testbed. The T&E team will be responsible for developing, planning, and running the end of Phase Capability Demonstrations and for assessing all performers against program metrics. Proposals should account for data sharing and interactions with the T&E team throughout all program Phases.

The City will notify Contractor that the contract will not be renewed if funding is unavailable or if the contractor's past performance is not within industry standards. Basis for Evaluation

Contract award shall be made to the lowest Responsive and Responsible Bidder based on the evaluation and award criteria established herein and subject to consideration of all factors identified in RCW 39.26 and preferences in state law.

Ecology reserves the right subject to the provisions of RCW 39.26.160 to: (1) Waive any informality; (2) Reject any or all Bids, or portions thereof; (3) Accept any portion of the items bid unless the Bidder stipulates all or nothing in their Bid; (4) Cancel a solicitation and re-solicit Bids; (5) Negotiate with the highest scoring Responsive and Responsible Bidder to determine if that Bid can be improved for the Purchaser; (6) Award on an all or none consolidated basis taking into consideration "lifecycle costs"; and, (7) Award in aggregate when in the best interest of the state.

Ecology seeks to acquire the goods/services that best meet the State's needs and offers best value in the interest of the state.

Responses will be evaluated on the basis of the information provided in the Bidder's Proposal. Bidders are not to assume that the evaluators know anything about the Bidder or its proposed solution. Therefore, it is important that the responses be clear and complete so that the evaluators can adequately understand all aspects of the Proposal. Failure of a Bidder to provide such may result in the Bidder receiving a low evaluation score or being deemed non-responsive.

The NAS, operated by the FAA Air Traffic Organization (ATO), is the most complex and busiest air traffic system in the world, overseen by the FAA. It is not merely the sky over the United States; it's a vast, integrated network of airspace, navigation facilities, equipment, services, airports, and the human and regulatory infrastructure required to manage civil, commercial, and military aviation safely and efficiently. It refers to the hardware and software that directly monitors or controls physical aviation assets. The core mission of the NAS is to protect persons and property on the ground and to ensure the safe, orderly, and efficient movement of over 45,000 flights and millions of passengers daily across 29 million square miles.
2.1.1 Airspace Operations
The NAS is divided into various classifications of controlled and uncontrolled airspace (Classes A, B, C, D, E, and G), each imposing specific requirements on pilots for communication, equipment, and flight procedures. All operations are governed by either Visual Flight Rules (VFR) for clear weather conditions or Instrument Flight Rules (IFR), which allow for flight in all weather and require strict separation services provided by ATC. Maintaining separation between aircraft, monitoring weather conditions, and providing pilots with essential aeronautical information like Notices to Airman (NOTAMs) are among the most critical daily functions.

1.5 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
The Period of Performance (PoP) for this contract will consist of a base year of one (1) year, and two (2) additional option periods, each structured as a six (6)-month extension. The base period will commence on July 1, 2026, and will end on June 30, 2027. Following the base period, the contract will include two (2) option periods, each lasting six (6) months, which may be exercised at the discretion of the contracting officer.

1.6 VERSION UPGRADE
The contractor shall provide the expertise necessary for the upgrade of Maximo Asset Management from one version to the next. Data migration is an essential part of the upgrade and is the responsibility of the contractors. Once the upgraded has been completed it is the contractor's responsibility to ensure end to end testing is conducted. Any error that results in Maximo being substantially or completely nonfunctional or inoperative the contractor will make reasonable efforts to correct or provide a work-around solution for errors. A final resolution to work-arounds shall be completed within 15 business days. The contractor shall prepare a work-up plan that will be evaluated and accepted prior to the start of the upgrade. The contractor shall submit to the Contracting Officer or the NIST Maximo Administrator by e-mail, the upgrade plan in an electronic Word document. The NIST Maximo Administrator shall review the upgrade plan and provide a written response within 10 working days. No work shall start on the actual upgrade until the plan is reviewed and approved.

M.2 TPS Evaluation Criteria and Their Relative Importance
Offeror proposals will be evaluated in three areas: Technical, Past Performance and Price. Technical and Past Performance, when combined (Non-Price), are significantly more important than Price. In the Non-Price areas, Technical and Past Performance are equal. The Technical areas are composed of three factors, listed in paragraph 1.0. Offerors should recognize that Price, although of lesser importance than Technical and Past Performance, might contribute substantially to the Source Selection Official's (SSO's) contract award decision. As the evaluation of competing offeror proposals in the Technical and Past Performance areas become more equal in rating, the more important Price will become in selecting the best value for the Government.

The Government will assign an adjectival rating to each of the factors/subfactors identified below as well as assigning an overall adjectival rating to each of the evaluation areas. The adjectival rating depicts how well the offeror meets the evaluation areas/factors/subfactors and solicitation requirements. The adjectival definitions are defined below:

EXCEPTIONAL: Offeror's technical proposal exceeds requirements of the solicitation in many areas. Benefits to the Government/ Bureau are identifiable.
VERY GOOD: Offeror's technical proposal meets requirements of the solicitation and exceeds requirements in some areas. Benefits to the Government/Bureau are identifiable. Problems may exist, but are minor, and may be resolved through discussions.
SATISFACTORY: Offeror's technical proposal meets the requirements of the solicitation. Some minor problems exist but can be resolved through discussions.
MARGINAL: Offeror's technical proposal does not meet some of the requirements of the solicitation. Contractor's technical proposal has identified problems that may result in a negative impact on service delivery (a negative impact on the Government). Problems are serious but can be resolved through discussions.
UNSATISFACTORY: Offeror's technical proposal fails to meet most of the requirements of the solicitation. Technical proposal has problems that will result in a negative impact on service delivery (a negative impact on the Government). Problems are serious, and it is not likely that they can be resolved through discussions.

1. Phased Contract Structure
This project shall be awarded as a two-phase contract.
Phase 1 shall commence July 1, 2026 and shall achieve completion no later than June 30, 2027.
Phase 2 is contingent upon funding approval by the Rappahannock Regional Jail Authority in January 2027. If funding is approved, Phase 2 shall commence July 1, 2027, and shall achieve completion no later than June 30, 2028.
The Authority reserves the right, at its sole discretion, to authorize Phase 2, modify its scope, or decline to proceed. No guarantee of Phase 2 award is expressed or implied.
Written authorization from the Authority shall be required prior to commencement of Phase 2 work.

The contract period shall be; Phase 1 shall begin July 1, 2026 with installation of the Camera System by June 30, 2027 to include a two-year contract for system maintenance. Upon availability of funds, Phase 2 shall begin July 1, 2027 with installation of the Security Control System by June 30, 2028. This phase shall include a one-year contract for system maintenance. Upon completion of the full scope of the project, a combined five-year contract for system maintenance shall begin to run July 1, 2028 and end June 30, 2033.

C.3.7.2 Incident Management (IcM) – Service Desk Procedures

The Contractor shall provide the Government access to a Service Desk. The Service Desk shall be responsive to the Government 24 hours a day, seven days a week. The Contractor shall document, develop, and follow Service Desk procedures using Incident Management/ Service Desk approaches as discussed in Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL). The Service Desk shall act as the central point of contact between Contractor's service providers and the Government users on a day-to-day basis. It is also a focal point for incident reports and for service requests. ITIL 2011 defines an incident as: An unplanned interruption to an IT Service or reduction in the quality of an IT service. Failure of a configuration item that has not yet affected service is also an incident — for example, failure of a module in the printer that has redundant capability such as the failure of a single laser module in a two-laser module printer. ITIL Incident Management manages the lifecycle of all Incidents. The primary objective of Incident Management is to manage the return of service to users as quickly as possible.

A Service Request is a user request for information, advice, or a standard change (a pre-approved change that is low risk, relatively common and follows a procedure e.g. a password reset (ITIL v3)).

The Incident Management process shall start within one hour of the time the Government contacts the Contractor Service Desk. Within four hours from the Government notification, the Contractor shall document and implement a mitigation strategy to remedy the issue. However, in cases where an Issuance Site does not have a serviceable personalization printer the service desk must ensure that actions are taken that return the site to operation within four hours.

2.4 HOTLINE SUPPORT AND PERFORMANCE MONITORING

Functionality and accuracy of the installed equipment will be continuously monitored by VA Logistics staff to ensure equipment operates according to the salient characteristics and provides accurate and timely information for inventory and reordering purposes. VA will notify the Contractor's Support Hotline of any issues via Contractor's support phone number or email.

The Contractor shall provide VA with a Support Hotline telephone number and email that VA can use to notify the Contractor of any issues with the installed equipment or software. The Support Hotline shall be staffed 7:00AM – 8:00PM EST and available 24x7x365 to provide support or assistance to VA. The Contractor shall respond to service calls no later than 24 hours after being notified of a problem with either equipment or installed software and resolve within 48 hours of notification by VA, any defects or functionality problems detected.

The Contractor shall work with VA onsite to replace supported hardware within 48 hours of notification of a problem if VA is not at fault for damage or defect. This assumes there is backup equipment onsite. If backup equipment is not onsite, the Contractor's support staff shall work with VA onsite to replace equipment when it arrives via Contractor's return merchandise authorization process.

The Contractor shall provide performance monitoring. The Contractor shall monitor the installed equipment and software remotely by daily check-ins that shall occur remotely at least four times every 24 hours for 12 months for each period of performance after installation completed. The Contractor shall notify VA and provide maintenance support in the event of a problem with equipment or software detected during the performance monitoring.

EVALUATION CRITERIA:

To be considered, respondents must have extensive experience in health care reimbursement and clinical research environments. Successful applicants will be able to demonstrate via their proposal, proposed team members, past experience and references, and in-depth knowledge of hospital management, a familiarity with operating costs for services provided within a clinical research environment.

Evaluation Criteria will be as follows:

40%	Proposed strategic approach to achieve required tasks and deliverables
20%	Demonstrated knowledge of and relevant past experience with clinical research settings, including practices of hospital cost management in clinical research settings.
20%	Proposed team members, including references.
20%	Cost to meet the SOW requirements.

An overall rating of each proposal meeting the minimum requirements will be developed using weighted combination of technical and price proposals. Price proposal will be considered on the basis of total price. The business proposals will be evaluated to determine the reasonableness of the proposed price. The lowest total evaluated price will be assigned 20 points. Points for all other offers will be calculated as follows:

Lowest Total Evaluated
------------------------------------------X 20 Points = Number of points to be assigned
Offeror's Total Evaluation Price

The Government intends to make a single award to the eligible, responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation, and is the most advantageous to the Government, cost and other factors considered. The Government will make an award to that offeror whose quotation provides the best overall value to the Government. The Government may also ask for oral presentations which will influence the final selection.

4.1 Deliverables. This program will be executed through a multi-track approach, leveraging specialized technical teams working concurrently to achieve both implementation and operational objectives. While Phases 1 and 2 focus on the foundational engineering and deployment of the security tool suite by an Implementation Team, Phase 3 represents the parallel and ongoing work of a distinct Operational Support & Sustainment Team.

4.1.1 Phase 1: Technical Tool Gap Analysis and Solution Strategy
4.1.1.1 Gap Analysis and Solution Recommendations: Assess the CDE to perform a technical gap analysis focused specifically on identifying the security tools and services required to complete the enterprise security suite. This analysis is not a formal PCI DSS assessment or scope-reduction exercise. The contractor shall deliver a formal report detailing recommendations for a complete tool suite (e.g., SIEM, DLP, MFA) and how these tools must integrate into a centralized reporting framework. The contractor is expected to collaborate with the Government's designated QSA firm but not to perform QSA functions.

4.1.1.2 High-Level Executive Summary: Deliver a summary of key findings and recommendations for the required security tools and services to comply with PCI DSS. The contractor must prioritize the use of modern, automated, and cloud-based tools and services.

4.1.1.3 Solution Implementation Strategy: The Contractor must deliver a Solution Implementation Strategy document. This document must serve as the foundational plan and must include recommendations for a complete enterprise tool suite to be deployed specifically within the CDE at each respective installation, a detailed cost analysis for all recommended tools, and a high-level implementation plan that defines the necessary steps, resource requirements, and a project timeline with a target initial completion date of August 2026.

Affordable Mass Cruise Missiles Flight Event_2 Capstone Demonstration (16 months after contract award or sooner)

Statements of Capability should specifically address the contractor's experience and ability to provide (either currently or via rapid modification of existing capabilities) to support the above demonstrations within 16 months after contract award.

The goal is to have multiple vendors (up to 5) to participate in the flight events. It is imperative for this effort that vendors collaborate with each other for a successful demonstration. The Capstone demonstration will consist of at least four (4) heterogenous NCA affordable mass cruise missiles, sharing information with each other across a data link and swarming to engage a target using an initial set of behaviors, receiving in-flight tactics re-programming based on a previous battle outcome, then re-engaging and prosecuting the target using the revised, battle-informed behaviors, all in a potentially GPS-challenged environment.

The Government assumes, by the nature of this effort, that platform teams are willing to coordinate with other platform teams and modify subsystems, if needed, to facilitate the multi-vendor, multi-platform demonstration and set the foundation for affordable mass and scalable, interoperable NCA operations in highly contested environments.
