3.2 PLACE OF PERFORMANCE

Much of the contract work will be performed at ICE detention facilities. The Government reserves the right to conduct remote inspections and reviews of facilities should on-site inspections not be able to occur due to an outbreak of a pandemic disease, adverse weather conditions, or any other situation that might prevent travel to facilities.

Contractor administrative functions (preparing, submitting, or transmitting reports) will be performed at the contractor's workspaces. Contractor personnel will not have workspace available at the detention facility or field office. Contractors should anticipate that no workspace or access to telephones, computers, or the Internet will be provided for contractor personnel on-site or at field offices.

Inspections, reviews, and investigative support will normally be performed on-site or remotely at ICE detention facilities in continental US (CONUS) locations, Puerto Rico, and outside continental US (OCONUS) locations; OCONUS locations may include Guam, the Virgin Islands, Alaska, and Hawaii.

The NITE-STAR Capability Development MA IDIQ is not a mandatory contract vehicle.

This IDIQ is centralized. SSC/TIK is the responsible contracting office for all task/delivery orders (TO/DOs) awarded under this contract vehicle.

TO/DOs awarded under this IDIQ contract are intended to support the objectives of the OTTI Program Executive Officer (PEO) and support will extend to other stakeholders within the space test and training enterprise.

This IDIQ contract requires the issuance of TOs/DOs to authorize work pursuant to FAR clause 52.216-18 Ordering.

An initial DO in the amount of $10,000, for a Post Award Kickoff Conference, will be awarded upon execution of each MA IDIQ Basic Contract. This initial TO will satisfy the requirements in DAFFARS 5316.504(a)(2). The monetary limit for the sum of all TO/DOs against these contract vehicles is $981,000,000.

The procedures for ordering are governed by FAR 16.505 Ordering. With the exception of the initial TO/DOs, the Contracting Officer will issue a Fair Opportunity Request for Offer (FORFO) to all prime contractors, unless a fair opportunity exception exists. TO/DOs will be subject to FAR 16.505(b)(1) fair opportunity processes with specific instructions provided for each FORFO.

It is anticipated that TOs/DOs will be selected on a best-value tradeoff or lowest price technically acceptable but the Government reserves the right to use any selection method for TOs/DOs.

3.1.1 Prohibition of Performer Participation from Federally Funded Research and Development Centers (FFRDCs) and other Government Entities
ARPA-H is primarily interested in responses to this solicitation from commercial performers, academia, non-profit organizations, etc. In certain circumstances, FFRDCs and Government Entities may have unique capabilities that are not available to proposing teams through any other resource. Accordingly, the following principles will apply to this solicitation.
• FFRDCs and Government entities, including federal Government employees, are not permitted to respond to this solicitation as a prime or sub-performer on a proposed performer team.
• If an FFRDC or Government entity has a unique research idea that is within the technology scope of this solicitation that they would like considered for funding; OR, if an FFRDC or Government entity, including a federal Government employee, is interested in working directly with the Government team supporting the research described by this solicitation, contact AIR@arpa-h.gov.
• If a potential prime performer believes an FFRDC has a unique capability without which their solution is unachievable, they may provide documentation as part of their Solution Summary submission demonstrating they have exhausted all other options. ARPA-H will consider the documentation to determine if inclusion of the FFRDC is necessary for the Solution.

7. EVALUATION FACTORS AND BASIS FOR AWARD
The Government will evaluate quotations to determine which vendors represent the best overall value for establishment of BPA(s).
The Government intends to establish up to two (2) BPAs with responsible ISBEE vendors whose quotations represent the best overall value to the Government, considering price and non-price factors.

7.2 Evaluation Factors (in descending order of importance)
1. Technical Capability and Approach
2. Relevant Experience and Past Performance
3. Quality Control and Compliance
4. Price
Price will be evaluated for reasonableness, completeness, and balance in relation to the technical quotation and the Government's requirements.

7.3 Best Value Tradeoff
All non-price factors, when combined, are significantly more important than price.
The Government will perform a comparative assessment of quotations to determine which quotation provides the best overall value.
The Government may select a higher-priced quotation if the additional technical merit, reduced performance risk, or demonstrated ability to meet or exceed the PWS performance standards (including 95% AQL requirements) justifies the price premium.

AFRL is seeking to demonstrate multiple weapons, employing interoperable and intelligent affordable mass networked collaborative autonomous (NCA) weapon salvos, capable of rapid software updates based on recent battle data, synergizing effects for target neutralization in a complex, contested battlespace. The goal is that multiple weapons provided from multiple vendors will work together to demonstrate initial proof-of-concept for multi-vendor, multi-platform interoperability appropriate for large-scale affordable mass cruise missile operations. An affordable mass cruise missile is defined, for this effort, as capable of traveling a minimum of 350 nautical miles, equipped with a weapon datalink, with a cost of less than $250K per unit.

3.4 Security and Privacy
Securing NASA data and commands, and the integrity of the overall system/service is mandatory. The NEXUS System will implement comprehensive cybersecurity, information assurance, cryptographic, and cloud security controls across the entire service architecture, encompassing space, ground, network, and cloud elements, including all prime contractor, subcontractor, service provider, hosted payload provider, ground segment operator, and third-party entity components. The provider will be required to complete the following activities and provide NASA with the artifacts called out in this section to enable NASA to determine the adequacy and acceptability of the provider's security control implementation.
Security Architecture Disclosure (CASQ): All candidates must complete the Commercial Architecture Security Questionnaire (CASQ) to ensure alignment with federal cybersecurity requirements.
Control Baseline and Evidence: The provider will provide evidence of cybersecurity control implementation satisfying High-impact requirements for availability and integrity consistent with FIPS 199 categorization. The provider shall implement and maintain information security controls in accordance with NIST SP 800-53 (current revision) consistent with FISMA and applicable NASA policy.
The system and associated services shall meet:
• High impact level controls for Integrity and Availability
• Moderate impact level controls for Confidentiality
The provider shall implement all required management, operational, and technical safeguards consistent with the applicable control baseline. The provider may demonstrate compliance using recognized industry cybersecurity frameworks or certifications (e.g., CMMC, ISO/IEC 27001, FedRAMP, or equivalent).

CLIN 3: Financial Management Oversight Reviews

The Contractor shall conduct financial management oversight reviews at the written request of the FTA COR. The following reviews may be requested:

CLIN 3.1 – Full Scope Financial Managements System Review

The objectives of this task are to initiate, perform, and report on financial management system reviews of recipients assigned by FTA. The reviews shall be performed in accordance with applicable AICPA Professional Standards. The contractor performs transaction testing to evaluate the recipient's financial management systems. The contractor determines if the financial management system is adequate to support effective control of capital projects at all project stages, provides a brief description of the recipient and its current major capital investment project, and verifies that the recipient's financial management system meets the requirements of the Uniform Administrative Requirements, as defined in 2 CFR Part 200. If a recipient's system is not in compliance with the Uniform Administrative Requirements, the contractor will identify which standard is not met and will identify the corrective actions needed, and the timeframe, to be in full compliance with the regulations.

During full scope FMO reviews, contractors conduct a series of interviews, full transaction reviews, and appropriate substantive tests to determine whether the recipient's financial management system meets the requirements of the Uniform Administrative Requirements (2 CFR part 200). The contractors then express an objective, external, independent professional opinion to FTA, in accordance with established public accounting standards, on the effectiveness of the recipient's internal control environment. These reviews require multiple visits to the recipient. At times, a visit to the cognizant FTA Headquarters or Regional Office is also required.

1.3.1.1 It is mandatory that the contractor provides at a minimum one (1) full-time on-site Program Manager (PM) to oversee and direct the contractor employees.
1.3.1.2 The PM will be responsible for learning the basic responsibilities of the three positions: Test Examiner, OPI/Web-based Test Scheduler and OPI Facilitator.
1.3.1.3 The PM will coordinate daily with both the section supervisors and the COR.
1.3.1.4 The PM must complete all orientation for their position and all orientations and certifications required for the Test Examiner position.
1.3.1.5 The PM will be present during the initial orientation of all employees.
1.3.1.6 The PM will also fill the position of OPI Facilitator when required.

1.3.2 Test Examiner:
1.3.2.1 The Test Examiner will administer, or proctor tests as directed by the PM. Each test session is defined as one AM or PM test session. The government will determine the number of sessions required each week. The number of sessions per week will not exceed 50. The COR will notify the contractor with the intended schedule no later than 14 days prior to the designated work unit. The maximum number of sessions in a year would be 2500 and the minimum 1250.

Q44. Are there specific proposal sections or volumes the Government expects offerors to submit (e.g., Technical Approach, Management, Past Performance), or is offeror-defined organization acceptable?
A44. The Offeror should ensure that each of the evaluation factors of 52.212-2 are provided, to have a real opportunity for award consideration: Factor 1, Technical Capability, Factor 2, Past Performance, and Factor 3, Price. Please note the details at 52.212-1(m), especially the directions to submit three (3) Volumes; it is important that no Pricing is included in the Technical Capability or Past Performance portions of the proposal, for evaluation purposes. Within the restrictions of the Solicitation (e.g. file format, email size), each Offeror controls discretion for submitting their best proposal.

Q45. Can the Government confirm the evaluation factors and their relative importance for this RFQ?
A45. Factor 1, Technical Capability, is more important than Factor 2, Past Performance. The non-price factors (Factors 1 and 2), when combined, are significantly more important than Factor 3, Price. However, the importance of price will increase if offers are considered essentially equal in terms of non-price factors. To be considered for award, a proposal must receive a rating of "Acceptable" or higher for Factor 1, Technical Capability; a rating of "Unacceptable" would be assigned to evaluations of an offer with a deficiency, or multiple weaknesses that are found to amount to a deficiency in aggregate. Please see FAR 52.212-2 of RFQ provided for full details.

2.4 SECURITY REQUIREMENTS: Personnel Security Clearances: All contractor personnel proposed to perform services under this acquisition, including development, implementation, project management, and on-site support, must have an active Top Secret/Sensitive Compartmented Information (TS/SCI) security clearance at the time of proposal or quotation submission. Personnel without the requisite clearance at the time of submission will not be considered. Responders must be prepared to provide verification of clearances upon request.

System Accreditation: The proposed solution must be capable of meeting the stringent security requirements of the Intelligence Community (IC) and the Department of Defense (DoD). The system will be required to undergo a full security assessment and authorization process in accordance with the Risk Management Framework (RMF) as defined in DoD Instruction 8510.01 and obtain Authority to Operate (ATO) on NGA networks.

Information Assurance Compliance: The solution must follow all applicable IC Directives (ICDs), including but not limited to ICD 503 ("Intelligence Community Information Technology Systems Security Risk Management"), as well as NGA-specific security policies and implementation guides.

Data Security: All data, both at rest and in transit, must be encrypted using FIPS 140-2 (or successor) validated cryptographic modules. The system must ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all data processed and stored.

Eligible Offeror: This is an unrestricted solicitation. Small businesses are encouraged to propose.

Cost Sharing or Matching: Cost Sharing is not required, but is allowed.

Federally Funded Research and Development Centers: The following guidance is provided for Federally Funded Research and Development Centers (FFRDCs) contemplating submitting a proposal, as either a prime or subcontractor. FAR 35.017-1(c)(4) prohibits an FFRDC from competing with any non-FFRDC concern in response to a Federal agency request for proposal for other than the operation of an FFRDC (with exceptions stated in DFARS 235.017-1(c)(4)). There is no regulation prohibiting an FFRDC from responding to a solicitation. However, the FFRDC's sponsoring agency must first make a determination that the effort being proposed falls within the purpose, mission, general scope of effort, or special competency of the FFRDC, and that determination must be included in the FFRDC's proposal. In addition, AFRL must make a determination that the work proposed would not place the FFRDC in direct competition with domestic private industry. Only after these determinations are made, would a determination be made concerning the FFRDC's eligibility to receive an award.

Government Agencies: If a Government agency is interested in performing work, contact the Program Manager identified in the BAA. If those discussions result in a mutual interest to pursue your agency's participation, the effort will be pursued independent of this announcement.

Other:

Section VI - Evaluation and Award
RRB Supplement to FAR 52.212-2 Evaluation of Offers
A. The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation, based on price and technical factors including past performance, to the responsible offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government. Offerors proposals shall be evaluated in compliance with FAR 52.212-2, Evaluation of Offers – Commercial Items (Incorporated by Reference) and the procedures outlined in VI.B through VI.E. below.
Best Value Evaluation. Technical evaluation shall be weighted significantly greater than the Price evaluation. Responses will be evaluated according to which firm best can meet the performance requirements of the statement of work as evidenced by their Approach, Quality Control and Staffing qualifications. Past performance, value added characteristics, etc. will be included to determine which proposal is the best value to the government.
B. Business Price Proposal evaluation –
1. The Government will evaluate Offeror's Total Evaluated Price (TEP) proposal as the sum of the offered firm fixed prices in the sub-CLINs (AA and AB) of CLINs: 0001 thru 0005. Note: The RRB shall evaluate pricing on the above optional sub-CLINs whether or not they are exercised per the FAR clause 52.217-5, Evaluation of Options (Jul 1990).
3. The RRB will consider any Offer not proposing pricing in sub-CLINS of CLINs 0001 thru 0005 OR not being able to perform all the required elements in the SOW as described in Section III, as non-responsive to the solicitation and the RRB will not further evaluate such an offer.

4.0 Requested Information:
NSWC PCD is seeking a qualified Consortium Manager (CM) firm to establish and administer this consortium. The CM will serve as the single point of contact for the Government for the OTA's administration and will be responsible for establishing and managing a consortium of traditional and non-traditional defense contractors, small businesses, and academic institutions. The CM will act as the single point of contact for the administration of the Other Transaction Agreement (OTA) and is expected to perform the following functions/key responsibilities include:
• Consortium Administration: Establish the legal and operational framework for the consortium, including a governing structure and membership agreements.
• Member Recruitment and Engagement: Market the consortium to attract, recruit, and retain a diverse and active membership, with a particular focus on engaging non-traditional defense contractors.
• Solicitation of Projects: Collaborate with the Government to develop and issue calls for white papers or proposals (e.g., Research Project Announcements) to the consortium membership to address specific Government requirements.
• Proposal Management: Receive and manage member responses to solicitations, ensuring a fair, transparent, and efficient evaluation process leading to Government award decisions. Collaborate with the Government to solicit proposals from members for specific prototype projects and conduct merit-based evaluations of proposals and make award recommendations.
• Project Award and Execution: Award, manage, and monitor all project-level agreements or sub-awards made to consortium members.
• Financial Management: Manage all financial aspects of the OTA, including the collection of membership fees (if applicable), distribution of project funding, and detailed financial tracking and reporting to the Government.

C.3.6 TASK 6: Transition (LH) (Optional)
When requested by the Government, the Contractor shall provide support to transition operation and maintenance of the eA3 Project systems, all or in part, to the Government and/or a new Contractor. The objective of this optional task is for the Contractor to completely turn over all documentation, information, and transfer knowledge to the Government and/or new contractor for assuming all or partial roles related to the eA3 application, software, and its associated systems. The Government estimates this Sub-Task shall take three months and must be completed 14 business days prior to the end of the Task Order period of performance. All Transition activities must follow IRM 2.126 Transition Management, which IRM will be provided by the Contracting Officer Representative (COR) to the Contractor if this Sub-Task is ordered.

If this optional task is exercised the Contractor shall develop a draft Transition Management Plan (TMP) that describes the management of the transition activities and present it to the Government within 15 business days after the Government has requested transition performance. The Government will have 5 business days to review the TMP Transition Plan and provide comments to the Contractor.

DESIRED OUTCOMES – The Contractor shall successfully create and implement a TMP to transition knowledge and documentation to enable the Government to continue operation and maintenance support. At the conclusion of the transition, the Government will have a clear understanding of how to effectively maintain the operation and maintenance of the eA3 Project systems with no interruption to or degradation in service.

The Government Estimated Program Ceiling and Periods of Performance for the CPOs are as follows:

CPO 1 – AFRL/AFOSR: $8.8M. The period of performance for CPO 1 will be a Base Period of 12-months plus four 12-month Option Periods for a total of 60 months, plus the optional six-month extension of services.

CPO 2.1 – USSF (SSC) - Colorado: $11.7M. The period of performance for CPO 2.1 will be a Base Period of 12-months plus four 12-month Option Periods for a total of 60 months, plus the optional six-month extension of services.

CPO 3 – AFCENT: $2.8M. The period of performance for CPO 3 will be a Base Period of 12-months plus four 12-month Option Periods for a total of 60 months, plus the optional six-month extension of services.

CPO 4 – NRO: $8.2M. The period of performance for CPO 4 will be a Base Period of 12-months plus four 12-month Option Periods for a total of 60 months, plus the optional six-month extension of services.

The Government anticipates an initial five CPOs to be awarded simultaneously with the award of the PIA. 4.5 Task 5: High Power Electronics
4.5.1. Power Electronic Circuits: The Contractor shall develop high performance, high efficiency power conversion circuits (including wireless power transfer) enabled by and implementing relevant wide bandgap power electronic materials and devices.
4.5.2. Modeling and Simulation: The Contractor shall develop semiconductor device models, utilize available simulation code, and apply each to novel wide bandgap semiconductor device structures.
4.5.3. Advanced Wide Bandgap Deposition and Characterization: The Contractor shall utilize advanced deposition techniques and optical/electrical techniques to characterize wide bandgap semiconductor materials and devices.
4.5.3.1: The Contractor shall utilize transient optical characterization techniques to characterize the properties of wide bandgap semiconductor materials and device.
4.5.3.2: The Contractor shall use Fourier Transform optical characterization techniques to characterize the properties of wide bandgap semiconductors.
4.5.3.3: The Contractor shall develop custom control software and hardware design and construction in support of wide bandgap semiconductor characterization efforts.
4.5.4: Wide Bandgap Semiconductor Device Fabrication: The Contractor shall use semiconductor deposition and processing techniques to fabricate a wide variety of wide bandgap semiconductor devices.

The FBOP Enterprise Tablet Initiative seeks to design, deploy, and sustain a secure, scalable, and compliant digital services platform that will provide approximately 150,000 users across 120 facilities nationwide with equitable access to education, healthcare, legal, religious, and communication resources. The program's purpose is to enhance service delivery while maintaining strict adherence to federal IT security and accessibility standards.

The initiative encompasses the full lifecycle of planning, procurement, installation, configuration, and sustainment of tablet devices and supporting infrastructure. This includes extending wired and wireless network access to the housing unit bed level and designated service areas (visitation, education, medical, psychology, religious, and recreational spaces) with explicit prohibitions on wireless bridging. All tablet devices will support modern Wi-Fi standards (IEEE 802.11a/b/g/n/ac, Wi-Fi 5, Wi-Fi 6, Wi-Fi 6E, and forward compatibility with Wi-Fi 7). All other equipment will support wired Ethernet connectivity (IEEE 802.3 over Cat6 cabling). Hosting will reside exclusively in GovCloud environments, with mandatory FedRAMP High certification and full FISMA compliance.

The solution must enable secure subscription-based access to streaming, downloadable content, and learning modules, while enforcing restrictions such as single-player gaming only. Additional services include AM/FM radio - television, mail scanning, and secure international and local calling capabilities. Telehealth (psychology and medical visits), religious programming, legal, and educational visits will be proctored or confined to approved designated locations. To ensure adherence to FBOP IT Standards the vendor must enable FBOP administrative oversight through role-based access controls with nested group management up to five layers deep, ensuring granular control across devices, groups, locations, and content.

MSCB is currently deployed and integrated within the Department of Defense Information Network (DODIN) and DISA requires the ability to maintain current systems while meeting future system and network capacity and performance requirements. The CBII solution must be interoperable and compatible with the DISN Joint Infrastructure (DJI) security stack located at DISA's ten global IAPs; must comply with DoW mandates for supporting IPv6 network traffic; and must adhere to risk management assessment and authorization requirements. The current capability, MSCB, complies with these requirements.

This follow-on effort focuses on maintaining and enhancing this system by continuing to utilize Menlo Security, Inc.'s cloud environment and adding smart, automated defenses (using artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML)) to proactively block cyberattacks, ensuring a strong and reliable defense against web-based threats. As of December 2025, DISA has successfully migrated 3.05M DoW users to the CBII environment with MSCB. This migration was implemented over 3.5 years and encompassed approximately 70 DoW organizations, which involved extensive coordination, training, testing, organizational collaboration, technical synchronization, and end-user validation. The complexity and scope of replacing the existing MSCB would result in major disruptions to DoW cyber operations, posing a significant threat to national security.

5.7.3 System Consolidation and Redesign

The Contractor shall implement a system consolidation and redesign of the PD&R KMSS.

The PD&R KMSS information architecture and visual design system must adopt an architecture model and design system complementary to www.HUD.gov. The design system must comply with Federal Government design system standards (https://designsystem.digital.gov/) unless approved by the Government in writing.

The Contractor shall provide a Draft system architecture, information architecture, and visual design system for the PD&R KMSS within 60 calendar days of Contract start.

The Contractor shall incorporate feedback from the Government and provide a Final information architecture and visual design system for Government approval within 30 calendar days of receiving comment.

The Contractor shall deploy the full system in with the new architecture and design within 330 calendar days of Contract start. If the Government directs cloud migration prior to system consolidation, the number of days for adoption of the design and architecture system is increased by the number of days allowed for cloud migration.

Deliverables
A035 One (1) information architecture and design system
A036 One (1) production deployment of systemwide redesign

9. Options structure — relationship among Base/Option Periods,
BAA (page 63) indicates:
• "It is anticipated that the award from this solicitation will be a multiple-year Cost Reimbursement type Completion contract with a Base of 1 year and two (2) option periods."
BAA Attachment 3 (pdf page 133) "Broad Agency Announcement Information" indicates:
• "NIAID estimates that the average annual total cost (direct and indirect costs combined) for a nonseverable base period and each subsequent, non-severable option period will average $2 million, for a total award of $6 million over three years per each successful offeror"; and
• "The period of performance proposed by an Offeror should not exceed 3 years."

Answer: Attachment 7 has been revised to remove in its entirety the language previously included under "Section 7: Options," including references to "Options 1–6 Extend the Term of the Contract" and "Options 7–41 Increase Level of Effort."
Please be advised that the contract structure and applicable Period of Performance are governed solely by Sections B and F of the BAA. As stated therein, the anticipated award is a Cost-Reimbursement, Completion-type contract consisting of a one (1) year Base Period and two (2) one-year Option Periods, for a total Period of Performance not to exceed three (3) years (Base plus Option 1 plus Option 2). There are no separate term-extension options beyond the two identified option periods and no standalone level-of-effort options under this solicitation.
Accordingly, offerors shall provide pricing only for the Base Period, Option Period 1, and Option Period 2, as set forth in Sections B and F.

J.6.2 RFTOP Instructions

Proposals shall comply with the requirements of FAR 52.212-1 (DEVIATION JAN 2026), which is hereby incorporated by reference, and the following addenda:

FAR 52.212-1 Addenda-Additional Instructions to Offerors—Commercial Products and Commercial Services

Offeror's proposals shall be organized in two volumes: Technical and Price.

Technical Volume:

The Technical Volume shall be submitted electronically, in Microsoft Word or searchable Adobe PDF.

The Technical Volume shall not exceed 15 total single-spaced pages with one-inch margins, using Times New Roman size 12-point font aligned to the left. A 10-point font may be used for charts, tables, and graphics. Any pages in excess of the applicable page limit will be disregarded, and will not be included in the evaluation. Failure of the Offeror to comply with the page limitations shall not constitute grounds for a protest.

(c) The following pages are excluded from the page count, font, margin, and spacing requirements:

Cover Page
Cover letter (maximum of one (1) page)
Table of Contents, List of Tables and Figures, Abbreviations and Acronyms

(d) The Technical Volume shall describe the Offeror's ability to provide data that meets all specifications required by the TO-1 Statement of Work (SOW). The Offeror shall propose separate capabilities for Part II, Sections II, III, and IV of the TO-1 SOW. Part II, Section I of the TO-1 SOW applies to all sections of Part II of the TO-1 SOW. The Technical Volume shall include a detailed explanation demonstrating the Offeror's capabilities to include the following:

Offerors must confirm that all proposed on-orbit capabilities will be completely operational and ready at the start of the Task Order 1 (TO-1) Period of Performance (PoP); if any proposed capability is not operational at the TO-1 PoP start date, the Offeror must submit a detailed plan and schedule to achieve readiness, covering at a minimum the launch manifest and the on-orbit check-out process, and guaranteeing that the Offeror will reach full operational status no later than six (6) months following the commencement of the TO-1 PoP.

9. Place of Performance

Work may be performed on-site, at all United States territories and possessions, and Non-United States territories and possessions Government facilities or at the Contractor's facility, sub-Contractor facility, supplier, or other designated locations (e.g., corporate, 3rd party, or sub-Contractor). The Government will, if required, further specify the Periodic Progress Meetings as required. On-site spaces at Government facilities may be revoked at any time per Government requirements.

Performance of this contract shall be conducted on a remote basis. However, the contractor must be available, on rare occasions, to report to a government facility to access the Secret Internet Protocol Router Network (SIPRNET) for the express purpose of updating SECRET-level documentation.

Place of Performance: Alternative work sites allow for regular telework or remote work flexibilities. Regardless of where work is performed, the Contractor shall ensure that work is completed effectively and that mission needs and requirements are met, without degradation of services. Also, the contractor may be required to account for the physical location of their personnel should this information be requested by the COR. The Contractor shall propose on-site rates for all work performed at an alternative work site. Incidental charges incurred while working at an alternative work site (e.g., electricity, internet) are not allocable to the contract and shall not be billed to the government.

Large Businesses, Emerging Large Businesses, Small Businesses, and Commercial-Sector Vendors:

Each LOE-Based QP will be scored as follows:
Offerors will earn 1,000 points per LOE-Based QP that has at least one (1) year of completed performance that has occurred within the last two (2) years prior to 01 May 2026.

2.2 Step Two (2) – TRADE-OFF

The trade-off factors below are listed in descending order of importance. Technical Acceptability is more important than Past Performance. Past Performance is more important than Price. Technical Acceptability and Past Performance, when combined, are significantly more important than Price. Price is not the most important evaluation factor, but its degree of importance will increase commensurably with the degree of equality among different Quoter's Technical and Past Performance quotes.

All Quoters will be evaluated for compliance with the RFQ requirements.

FACTOR 1: TECHNICAL ACCEPTABILITY
Technical Acceptability will be evaluated for the Quoter's demonstrated approach and understanding of the requirements with supporting rationale using the table below. Overall demonstrated understanding will include a review for reasonableness, feasibility, completeness, and risk to the Government. Technical Acceptability will be assigned one overall rating of 'Outstanding', 'Acceptable', or 'Unacceptable' based on the evaluation of all elements collectively.

FACTOR 2: PAST PERFORMANCE
Past performance will be rated as Acceptable, Neutral, or Unacceptable using the table below. The Government will award a Purchase Order resulting from this RFQ to the Quoters with Acceptable or Neutral past performance.

FACTOR 3: PRICE
The Quoter's technical submission will be evaluated for price reasonableness, unbalanced pricing, and completeness. Normally, adequate price competition establishes a fair and reasonable price.

TASK 18 - Technical Data Rights.

The contractor is solely responsible for negotiating all license agreements with third-party vendors. The Government will have no contractual or financial obligations to the vendors and will not be bound by the terms of any license agreements between the contractor and third-party vendors. This applies to all agreements, both those in place at contract award and any future agreements for the life of this contract. Within 60 days of contract award, the contractor shall deliver: (1) a detailed list of all hardware and software components, including Commercial-off-the-Shelf (COTS) and Government-off-the-Shelf (GOTS) software, to be used in performing the tasks under this contract; (2) a list of all third-party proprietary software, including open-source software, to be used in performing the tasks under this contract; and (3) identification of (a) any commercial software requiring modification or reconfiguration to meet government specifications or operate in the government environment and perform the tasks under this contract, (b) any GOTS and COTS software that will be integrated to perform the tasks under this contract, including the scope of the integration, and (c) whether the GOTS software can be segregated and used independently from the COTS software.

DCSA seeks to leverage Mentor-Protégé Agreements (MPAs) to mature Protégé firms for its critical mission spaces. By incentivizing Mentors to provide technical assistance, the agency aims to integrate high-capability small businesses into the Defense Industrial Base. This expansion directly strengthens national security by ensuring a resilient, value-added supply chain capable of safeguarding sensitive defense assets.

The core technical priorities for DCSA are structured around modernizing technology, integrating data, and securing IT infrastructure:

Modernizing IT Infrastructure and Cloud Optimization

National Background Investigation Services (NBIS): A primary focus is accelerating the delivery of NBIS to transform personnel vetting, including full transition to NBIS for Personnel Vetting operations including background investigations.

Cloud Migration: Transitioning systems, such as the Defense Information System for Security (DISS), to a secure, self-hosted cloud environment and transforming it to enhance scalability and agility.

System Modernization: Replacing legacy, slow-performing and siloed systems with modern, high performing components based on the DoW mandated Modular Open Systems Architecture (MOSA) approach.
